title: ADUs, Casitas, and Guest Houses in Arizona: The Complete Guide
summary: An accessory dwelling unit (ADU), casita, or guest house is a second living space on your lot, and which one you can build depends on Arizona's 2024 ADU laws and your city's rules. This guide covers what each term means, the statewide ADU mandate, per-city rules in Phoenix, Scottsdale, Mesa, and the northeast towns, cost, renting it out and the TPT tax, placement and design, the RV-garage casita, and the HOA and permit hurdles.
bodyHtml:
ADUs, Casitas, and Guest Houses in Arizona: The Complete Guide

One small room decides everything: the kitchen. Add one, and your backyard building becomes a home the law will let you rent. Leave it out, and you have a very nice bedroom instead. That single line runs through every question people ask us about second living spaces, from what to call the thing to whether it can pay for itself. An accessory dwelling unit (ADU) is a full home in miniature: its own kitchen, bath, and front door. A casita is the Southwest name for a small guest house, kitchen or no kitchen. A guest house with no kitchen is not a separate dwelling at all. Then, in 2024, the rules shifted under everyone's feet. A new state law, A.R.S. 9-461.18, told the bigger cities they had to allow at least one ADU on most single-family lots. Backyard homes that were flat-out banned a couple of years ago are getting approved now. Here is how it all fits together: what you can build, where it goes, what it costs, and the rules on renting, placement, and design. Every branch links to a page that goes deeper.

What this guide covers

  • ADU vs. casita vs. guest house: the definitions
  • Arizona's statewide ADU law
  • Per-city ADU and casita rules
  • Setbacks, size, and how many you can build
  • What an ADU or casita costs
  • Renting it out and the TPT tax
  • Placement and design in your plan
  • The RV-garage casita
  • HOA limits and the permit hurdles
  • Property taxes and cooling

ADU vs. casita vs. guest house: the definitions

People use these three words like they mean the same thing. They do not, and the mix-up can cost you. The word you land on decides whether you can rent the space, which rules apply, and how the county taxes it. An ADU is the full package: kitchen, bathroom, a place to sleep, and its own entrance. That makes it a real second home you can legally rent out. A casita is the Arizona word for a small guest house, and it may or may not have a kitchen. A guest house with no kitchen is not an independent dwelling. That keeps it clear of some rules, but it also means you cannot rent it as its own unit.

Start with what is an ADU in Arizona? and the side-by-side casita vs. guest house vs. ADU in Arizona . The kitchen question is settled in can I build a guest house without a kitchen in Arizona? . The glossary defines a casita and the junior ADU (JADU) , a smaller ADU type carved out of the main house.

Arizona's statewide ADU law

For years, whether you could build a backyard home came down to your city's mood. A 2024 law changed that. It sits at A.R.S. 9-461.18 for cities and A.R.S. 11-810.01 for counties, and it forces most larger cities to allow at least one ADU on single-family lots. The city rule kicks in above a certain population, and it puts a leash on how strict a city can get. Cities can no longer demand that you live on-site, and they can only ask for so much parking. This is the biggest shift in Arizona ADU law in a generation. It is the reason a casita your neighbor was denied in 2023 might sail through today.

Learn the requirements in Arizona ADU requirements and check whether your city is covered in which Arizona cities are required to allow ADUs? . For the statutes themselves, the reference library carries A.R.S. 9-461.18 (municipal ADU law) and A.R.S. 11-810.01 (county ADU law) .

Per-city ADU and casita rules

The state law is a floor, not the whole house. It says a city has to let you build, but the city still writes the fine print: how far off the property line, how tall, what it looks like, and whether you can list it on a nightly rental site. So the answer really does change from one town to the next. Phoenix, Scottsdale, and Mesa each wrote their own ADU ordinance to fall in line with the state. The smaller northeast towns of Cave Creek, Fountain Hills, and Carefree play by their own rules again, since they sit on bigger lots. Three numbers move the most from city to city: the biggest size allowed, the required setbacks, and whether you can rent the unit short-term.

See the big-city rules in ADU and casita rules in Phoenix , ADU and casita rules in Scottsdale , and ADU and casita rules in Mesa . For the northeast towns, read do Cave Creek, Fountain Hills, and Carefree allow casitas and ADUs? .

Setbacks, size, and how many you can build

Here is where a dream casita meets the tape measure. Three numbers tell you whether it even fits: how far it has to sit from the property lines, how big it can be, and how many units your lot is allowed. Most Arizona cities cap an ADU at a slice of the main home's floor area or a flat square footage, hold it back from the side and rear lines, and limit how many extra units one lot can carry. The state law nudged some of those limits your way. The exact figures, though, still come straight from your city's zoning code, so run them before you fall in love with a layout.

Work the dimensions with ADU setback and size rules by city in Arizona and the unit count with how many ADUs per property in Arizona? . The glossary explains the setback and lot coverage limits that constrain placement.

What an ADU or casita costs

People expect a casita to feel like a bonus room on the price sheet. It does not. It is a small house, and it wants everything a house wants: its own foundation, its own roof, and its own lines for power, water, and sewer or septic. That is why the cost per square foot usually lands higher than the main home. A kitchen, a bath, and the utility hookups cost about the same whether the building is big or small, and here they get spread over far fewer square feet. What swings the total the most is the site work, whether the unit is attached or standing on its own, and how nice you finish it.

Price a detached casita with cost to build a casita in Arizona and an independent ADU with cost to build an ADU in Arizona . If you are adding one to an existing home, see how to add a casita or detached structure to an existing home in Arizona .

Renting it out and the TPT tax

This is the part where a casita starts paying you back. In most Arizona cities you can rent a true ADU by the year or by the night. Rent it by the night and the state expects its cut: you owe Arizona's transaction privilege tax (TPT) and you need a license to collect it. A guest house with no kitchen usually cannot be rented as its own place, which is the plain reason to spring for a full ADU if income is the point. And cities get a say too. They can put rules on short-term rentals through licenses and zoning, even where the state stops them from banning rentals outright. So check two boxes before you count on the money: the city rule and the tax registration.

Start with can I rent out my casita or ADU in Scottsdale? and the conversion path in can I convert a guest house into a rentable ADU in Arizona? . Handle the tax with do I need a TPT license for a short-term rental in Arizona? .

Placement and design in your plan

A casita in the wrong spot works against you. Your mother-in-law hears every car door, or your own patio loses its afternoon light to a wall you built. Put it where the guest gets real privacy and the main house keeps its yard, its light, and its views. In a new custom home you have room to play: the casita can sit across a courtyard, off the motor court, or up over the garage. Each spot changes the privacy, the length of the plumbing runs, and how the two buildings share the outdoor space between them. One rule catches people every time. Any bedroom in a casita has to pass the building code's egress rule, meaning a window or door big enough to climb out of, or for a firefighter to climb in through. Miss it and the plan reviewer sends the whole set back.

Place it well with where should you place a casita or guest suite in a custom home layout? . Avoid the egress rejection explained in why did the plan reviewer reject my casita or guest house bedroom egress? . For where a detached casita fits the broader plan, see our custom home design guide .

The RV-garage casita

Say you own a motorhome and you want your kids to have somewhere to stay when they visit. You can have both in one building. Arizona lets you put living quarters above or beside an RV garage, so the coach parks downstairs and the guests sleep upstairs. It is a favorite on the bigger lots of Rio Verde, Cave Creek, and Apache Junction, where owners want a tall bay for the rig and a real guest suite under the same roof. Sharing that roof does not buy the casita a pass, though. The living part still has to meet the ADU or guest-house rules for size, setbacks, and egress, just like a casita standing on its own.

See how it works in can you build a casita above or beside an RV garage in Arizona? . The glossary defines the RV garage and the casita that pairs with it.

HOA limits and the permit hurdles

You can get the green light from the state, the green light from the city, and still hear no from the one board that meets in a clubhouse. An HOA can say no to a backyard dwelling even when everyone above it says yes. Plenty of Arizona planned communities limit detached buildings, rentals, or design through their CC&Rs and a design review committee, and those private rules stack right on top of the public zoning code. So if a homeowners association runs your lot, the city permit is only half the fight. Ask the HOA first, before you spend a dime on plans.

Settle the conflict with can my HOA block an ADU if the city allows it in Arizona? . The glossary explains the CC&Rs and the design review committee that enforce them. The HOA review process for any custom build is covered in how does HOA design review affect building a custom home? .

Property taxes and cooling

A casita comes with two bills that show up long after the crew leaves. The first is your property tax. New livable, permitted square footage lands on the county assessor's books, your assessed value ticks up, and the tax follows. It is usually a small bump next to what the unit cost to build, but it is real and it comes every year. The second is the air conditioning. A guest house you use a few weeks a year should run on its own zone or its own system. Otherwise you spend all summer cooling an empty room, and in an Arizona August that adds up fast.

See the tax effect in does adding a casita or addition raise my property taxes in Arizona? and the cooling solution in how do you cool a casita or guest suite separately from the main house in Arizona? .

The takeaway

So it all comes back to that one small room. The kitchen tells you what your building legally is and whether it can earn its keep. The 2024 law swung the door open by making larger cities allow at least one ADU on most lots, but your city, your HOA, and the code's egress and setback rules still draw the real lines. Every section above links to the page with the exact numbers, and the broader custom home design guide shows how a casita drops into a whole plan. Treat it like the real house it is, get the city and HOA to say yes before you start, and it can give you extra room, a little rental income, or a soft landing for family as the years go by.

Sources

  • A.R.S. 9-461.18 (Accessory dwelling units; municipalities; the statewide ADU mandate)
  • A.R.S. 11-810.01 (Accessory dwelling units; counties)
  • City of Scottsdale: Accessory Dwelling Units (development standards)

title: Building a Custom Home in Apache Junction: The Complete Local Guide
summary: Building a custom home in Apache Junction means designing to the city's 2018 I-codes and 2017 NEC, paying a flat $6,736 development impact fee plus a valuation-based permit fee, and working a desert lot on the Pinal and Maricopa County edge. Expect about $225 to $400 per square foot in 2026, with land cheaper than north Scottsdale. The flat impact fee and the lower lot cost shape the budget.
bodyHtml:
Here is the good news for anyone priced out of north Scottsdale: the land out here costs a lot less, and the city keeps its fees simple. That combination is what makes an Apache Junction build pencil out. The tricky part is the map. Draw a line where Pinal and Maricopa counties meet, and Apache Junction sits right on top of it. So the very first thing to nail down on a desert parcel is which county it belongs to, because the county, not the city, signs off on your well and septic.

Think of this guide as your Apache Junction home base. Every section gives you the short answer, then points you to a deeper page where you can check the exact rules and numbers for your own lot.

What this guide covers

  • Cost to build in Apache Junction
  • Adopted building codes
  • The flat impact fee and permit fees
  • Single-residence zoning and setbacks
  • Desert lots and the Pinal-Maricopa edge
  • Well and septic on a desert lot
  • Designing for heat, soil, and monsoon
  • Inspections and certificate of occupancy
  • Power, access, and rural utilities
  • Builder, financing, and the construction loan
  • Buying the lot and starting the build
  • Common Apache Junction questions

Cost to build in Apache Junction

Plan on roughly $225 to $400 per square foot to build here in 2026. On top of that, the city collects a flat $6,736 development impact fee for a single-family home and a building permit fee based on the home's value, both paid before your permit issues. So why is this cheaper than the north metro? Not the labor and materials. Those cost about the same across the region. It comes down to the lot. Land here runs well below north Scottsdale, and that gap is most of the savings. For the full breakdown, see how much it costs to build a custom home in Apache Junction and, for the metro baseline, cost to build a house per square foot in Arizona .

Adopted building codes (2018 I-codes)

A building code is really just the rulebook your plans get graded against. Apache Junction uses the 2018 IRC and the rest of the 2018 I-code family, plus the 2017 National Electrical Code. The City Council adopted them on June 18, 2019, they took effect July 18, 2019, and they live in Chapter 7 of the City Code. That puts the city in step with much of Pinal County and one cycle behind Phoenix, which now runs the 2024 codes. Those editions do not always agree. A 2018 rule and a 2024 rule can call for different framing, wiring, or energy specs. So a plan set drawn for a Phoenix lot is not a safe bet to drop straight onto a lot out here.

And once again, the county line decides a lot. Before you count on any of these codes, pin down whether the parcel is inside city limits or out in unincorporated Pinal or Maricopa County. That answer tells you which rulebook applies and which office reviews your plans. For the full code list and the local edits, see Apache Junction adopted building codes and Arizona building code editions by city .

The flat impact fee and permit fees

An impact fee is the city's way of charging a new home for its share of roads, parks, and services. In Apache Junction it is a single flat number: $6,736 for a new single-family home. Break it apart and you get $3,250 for streets, $1,707 for parks, $1,229 for police, and $550 for the library, all collected at your first building permit. The building permit fee is a separate charge, set from the home's value using the ICC Building Valuation Data table. State law keeps these fees honest: under A.R.S. 9-463.05, a city can only charge for infrastructure that growth actually drives. The flat number makes budgeting easy, but check the current figures before you lock them in.

Here is the part worth knowing. Because the fee is flat, it does not climb with the size of your house. A valuation-based fee would. So the bigger the home, the more square feet you have to spread that fixed $6,736 across, and the smaller it feels per foot. See Apache Junction building permit and impact fees , the impact fees glossary entry , and building permit cost in Arizona .

Single-residence zoning and setbacks

Your lot lines are not the same as the space you can actually build on. Setbacks carve out a smaller box inside them, and that box is what your house has to fit. Apache Junction's single-residence districts run from RS-GR on 1.25-acre lots all the way down to RS-5 on 5,000-square-foot lots, and Table 5-2 spells out the yards for each. RS-GR asks for a 40-foot front, 20-foot sides, and a 40-foot rear. RS-7 tightens that to a 20-foot front, 7-foot sides, and a 20-foot rear. Find your district before you start designing, and you will know the real footprint you have to work with.

The roomy RS-GR yards fit the big desert lots on the edge of town. The tighter RS-7 and RS-5 yards belong to the platted subdivisions. For every district's exact yards, coverage, and height, see Apache Junction residential zoning and setbacks . For corner parcels, see corner lot and double frontage setbacks in Arizona , and for the general ceiling, how tall a custom home can be in Arizona .

Desert lots and the Pinal-Maricopa edge

Picture two lots a mile apart on the edge of town. One flat, one tilting up toward the Superstitions. Same city, but they can land in different counties the moment you step outside city limits, and the sloped one will cost more to prep. Desert ground rarely comes build-ready. Grading, drainage, and access all add up before a single wall goes up, and a hillside parcel needs more of that work than a flat one. The county the lot sits in also settles where you file for well and septic. So on any edge parcel, the opening question is always the same one: which jurisdiction? See how to know if your lot is in the city or unincorporated county .

Well and septic on a desert lot

Step off the city grid and your home has to make its own water and handle its own waste. That means two separate approvals: a septic permit, and a private well the state signs off on through an ADWR Notice of Intent to Drill. Where you file depends on the county. On a Pinal County parcel, the septic permit comes from the Pinal County Aquifer Protection Division, a job ADEQ hands down to the county, and on lots of 5 acres or fewer that same county reviews the well. A Maricopa County parcel files septic through Maricopa County Environmental Services instead. Either way, the septic clock starts with a soils evaluation and a perc test.

Plenty of lots inside the city already have municipal water and sewer waiting at the street, and that skips the whole well-and-septic step. On a desert parcel it does not, and those two approvals are the ones most likely to slip your schedule. So start them early. See Pinal County septic and well requirements , Maricopa County septic and well requirements , whether you need a soil test to build a house in Arizona , and how close a well can be to a septic system .

Designing for heat, soil, and monsoon

Out here, three forces write the first draft of your house before you ever pick a paint color: heat, expansive soil, and monsoon storms. Every serious build at the east edge of the metro answers to them. The heat comes first. Weeks of 110-plus days push you toward deep roof overhangs, glass that blocks the sun, heavy insulation, and a high-SEER heat pump sized for the real load. Then the ground. Many desert lots sit on soil that swells when it is wet and shrinks when it dries, so you want a soils report and an engineered foundation, often a post-tensioned slab, to keep the concrete from cracking as the earth moves under it. And the rain. July and August storms hit fast and can sheet right off the Superstition foothills, so your drainage has to shove that water away from the slab, not toward it.

These forces shape the bones of the home more than the floor plan ever will. Get a soils report early, because it tells you which foundation the lot needs before you put a price on the build. See whether you need a soil test to build a house in Arizona and the expansive soil glossary entry .

Inspections and certificate of occupancy

The city checks your home in stages as it goes up, from the foundation to the final walkthrough, and each stage has to pass before the crew can cover that work with the next. Clear the final inspection and the city hands you a Certificate of Occupancy, the document that says the home is legal to live in. On a desert lot with a well and septic, add two more sign-offs: the septic system and the water setup, both cleared by the county on its own track, separate from the city's building inspections. The permit submittals and the field process are in Apache Junction residential building permit requirements and the Apache Junction inspections process . The finish line is the certificate of occupancy .

Power, access, and rural utilities

Stand on a raw desert lot and look for the power pole. There may not be one at the property line, and that changes your budget. Running power to the parcel and cutting in a driveway can add real money and weeks of lead time before the house even starts. Treat the power run, the meter spot, and a graded access road as early-schedule work, right alongside the well and septic, not something you sort out later. See how much it costs to extend power or utilities to a rural Arizona lot , how temporary construction power and the meter spot work , and whether you need a will-serve letter to build in Arizona .

Buying the lot and starting the build

Do your homework before you buy, not after. Four things about the lot, its county, its zoning, its water source, and its utility access, set both how much house you can build and what it will cost. So check them first. Start with the county question, because that one decides the code you follow and where your septic gets filed. Then know that the lot itself needs paperwork before a footing pours: usually a grading or earthwork permit, a dust-control permit, and a call to 811 before anyone digs. See how to buy land to build a house in Arizona , how to know if your lot is in the city or unincorporated county , grading or earthwork permit and SWPPP , dust-control permit , 811 Blue Stake before digging , how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC , and construction-to-permanent loan in Arizona .

Builder, financing, and the construction loan

Line up your builder and your money before the design is final, because both of them shape it. A lender's appraisal and draw schedule pull on your budget and your timeline, so you want them settled early. And before you sign with any builder, run the license through the Arizona Registrar of Contractors. That ROC license is what stands behind the implied warranty on your home, so it is not a box to skip. Most custom homes run on a construction-to-permanent loan: it pays the build out in draws, then rolls into a regular mortgage when you finish, so you close once instead of twice. See how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC and the construction-to-permanent loan in Arizona .

Common Apache Junction questions

Most people who call us ask three things first: what it costs per foot, how the flat impact fee works, and how long the whole thing takes. Start with how much it costs to build a custom home in Apache Junction and building permit cost in Arizona . To see the whole project from start to keys, read the steps to building a custom home in Arizona and how long it takes to build a custom home in Arizona .

Sources

The framing facts in this guide come from the City of Apache Junction Development Services Fees . The cap on development fees is set in Arizona Revised Statutes 9-463.05 , septic on rural land runs through Pinal County Wells and Septic , and Arizona's broader code-adoption picture is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona .

This guide is general information, not a code or zoning interpretation. Fees, editions, and zoning rules change, so confirm the current requirements for your parcel with the City of Apache Junction and the right county before you rely on them.

Sources

  • City of Apache Junction Development Services Fees
  • Arizona Revised Statutes 9-463.05 (Development fees)
  • Pinal County Wells and Septic (Aquifer Protection)
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona

title: Building a Custom Home in Carefree: The Complete Local Guide
summary: Building a custom home in Carefree means designing to the town's 2018 I-codes and 2017 NEC, putting in a private well or hauled water and a septic system on most lots, and fitting a home inside deep rural yards under a strict 24-foot height cap. Expect about $350 to $700 per square foot in 2026, with desert estates passing $850. The lot and any design-review community drive the cost.
bodyHtml:
Buyers fall for Carefree the same way every time. They stand on a wide desert lot, look out at the boulders and the mountain light, and picture the house. What they rarely picture is the well, the septic field, and the rule that keeps the whole home under 24 feet. Those are the things that actually shape a Carefree build. The land is beautiful, and the land is also the budget.

This guide is your Carefree hub. Each section gives you the short answer first, then points you to the deep page where you can pin down the exact rules and numbers for your lot. One thing to settle before anything else: Carefree runs older codes than newer neighbors like Cave Creek and Fountain Hills, so the rules can flip depending on which town line your lot sits inside.

What this guide covers

  • Cost to build in Carefree
  • Adopted building codes
  • Permit and impact fees
  • Rural zoning, setbacks, and the 24-foot height cap
  • Carefree Ranch and Lone Mountain design review
  • Well and septic on a Carefree lot
  • Desert and hillside site work
  • Inspections and certificate of occupancy
  • Power, access, and rural utilities
  • Buying the lot and starting the build
  • Common Carefree questions

Cost to build in Carefree

Plan on $350 to $700 per square foot in 2026, and $850 and up for a high-end desert estate. But the per-foot number only tells you what the finished house costs. It leaves out the parts that make Carefree, Carefree. Drilling a well or trucking in water, putting in a septic system, carving a pad out of a rocky slope, and setting the house back inside the town's deep yards all pile onto the total before you count a single tile. In a place like Carefree Ranch, add plan fees, building-envelope fees, and a height cap that quietly limits your options. Two people can spend the same per foot and walk away with wildly different bills. For the full breakdown, see how much it costs to build a custom home in Carefree and, for the metro baseline, cost to build a house per square foot in Arizona .

Adopted building codes (2018 I-codes)

Codes may sound like fine print, but they decide how your house gets framed, wired, and inspected, so they are worth a minute. Since January 24, 2023, Carefree builds to the 2018 IRC and the rest of the 2018 I-code family, plus the 2017 National Electrical Code, adopted by Ordinance 2022-02 and written into Town Code Section 10-1-2. That leaves the town a cycle or two behind its neighbors. Cave Creek, right next door, runs the 2024 codes and 2023 NEC. So a set of plans drawn for a Cave Creek lot is not automatically legal on a Carefree lot, and the reverse holds too. Watch one snag: an old town handout once listed a 1994 plumbing code, but the codified Town Code adopts the 2018 IPC, and the code is what wins.

Because Carefree's editions differ from the county around it and from the newer towns next door, the very first thing to nail down on any parcel is which jurisdiction it actually sits in. That one answer sets your code, your permit office, and your inspections. For the full code list and the per-code amendments, see Carefree adopted building codes and Arizona building code editions by city . For the plain-language version, see what building codes apply in Carefree .

Permit and impact fees

Carefree does not charge a flat permit price. It charges by construction valuation, meaning the fee scales with what the town figures your home is worth to build, with the math and amendments spelled out in Town Code Section 10-1-2. Here is the catch: the valuation table the town posts can be out of date, so the number you find online may not be the number you pay. Confirm the live fee before you lock a budget. See Carefree building permit and impact fees and, for how valuation pricing stacks up against per-foot cities, Arizona building permit fees: valuation vs per square foot . For the metro-wide picture, see building permit cost in Arizona .

Rural zoning, setbacks, and the 24-foot height cap

Your lot lines are not your buildable area. Carefree keeps homes well back from the edges with deep yards, and it caps height at 24 feet in every district. On Rural-70 land you owe a 60-foot front and rear yard, 30-foot sides, and can cover only 13 percent of the lot. Rural-43 eases up to 40-foot front and rear at 17 percent. The smaller R1-18 asks for a 30-foot front, 10-foot sides, and 25 percent coverage. The town's main residential districts run all the way from Rural-190, at more than four acres, down to R1-10 at 10,000 square feet.

That 24-foot cap is the rule that catches people off guard, because most nearby cities let you go to 30. It changes the shape of the house you can build. Pair the low ceiling with those deep front and rear yards on a big lot, and Carefree quietly pushes you toward one of two homes: a long single level that spreads across the land, or a two-story massed carefully to duck under the line. Learn your district before you draw a floor plan and you learn the real box you get to build in. For every district's exact yards, coverage, and height, see Carefree residential zoning and setbacks . For corner parcels, see corner lot and double frontage setbacks in Arizona , and for the general ceiling, how tall a custom home can be in Arizona .

Carefree Ranch and Lone Mountain design review

Buy in Carefree Ranch in the Village of Lone Mountain and you answer to two masters, not one. A design review committee (DRC) layers its own rules on top of the town's. Carefree Ranch holds you to 24 feet above natural grade at every point, wants the house broken into building masses of 16 to 17.5 feet, and sets 60-foot street and 35-foot side setbacks. You have 549 days to finish the exterior and pay roughly $1,500 in plan and building-envelope fees. None of this replaces the town permit. It rides alongside it, so you clear both to build.

The measuring rule is where people get burned. The 24 feet is checked at any point above natural grade, not just at the lowest corner of the house. On a downhill lot that means the roof has to step down with the slope instead of running flat, or the high side blows past the limit. So the terrain, not just your taste, argues for a massed, terraced design over a single tall box. For the full DRC rule set, see Carefree Ranch (Village of Lone Mountain) design guidelines . For how private review works in general, see how HOA design review affects building a custom home , how much HOA design-review fees run , how tall a home can be in a gated or hillside community , and whether you must use an approved builder list .

Well and septic on a Carefree lot

There is no city sewer pipe waiting at the curb out here. Most Carefree lots make their own water and handle their own waste. That means a septic system permitted by Maricopa County Environmental Services, plus either a private well authorized by the Arizona Department of Water Resources or a hauled-water setup with a storage tank on site. The septic side kicks off with a soils evaluation and a perc test. The well side kicks off with a Notice of Intent to Drill filed with ADWR. Each one runs on its own clock, and neither is quick, so start them early.

These are the two approvals most likely to blow up a Carefree timeline, and it is because neither one goes through the town. A perc test can fail outright, or pass but demand a bigger, pricier septic field on tight desert soil. A well can come in weak, or dry. That is why smart builders confirm water and soils before the design is final. A bad result can move the whole building pad, and you would rather learn that on paper than after the plans are drawn. See Maricopa County septic and well requirements , whether you need a soil test to build a house in Arizona , and how close a well can be to a septic system .

Desert and hillside site work

Before a single wall goes up, the raw desert has to be tamed. Carefree lots slope, and sloped desert means grading, drainage, retaining walls, and access roads, all of which cost real money up front. A hillside parcel needs engineered foundations and cut-and-fill work a flat lot simply skips, and the town's drainage rules dictate how water moves across your site plan. Then the 24-foot sloping-height cap in Carefree Ranch comes back into play, because the ground you sit on decides where the house can go and how tall it can rise. See how it hits the budget in how much it costs to build in Carefree , and how it hems in your buildable area in Carefree residential zoning and setbacks .

Inspections and certificate of occupancy

A custom home gets checked at every major step, from the first footing to the final walk. Each stage has to pass before the crew can cover it up, so an inspection you miss is work you may have to tear back open. You book inspections through the town's Permit and License Portal, and the request has to be in by 4 p.m. to land on the next available day. When the final inspection passes, the town hands over a Certificate of Occupancy, the paper that says you can move in. On a well-and-septic lot there is a second track: the county signs off on the septic system and the water setup on its own, apart from the town's building inspections. The permit submittals and the field process are in Carefree residential building permit requirements and the Carefree inspections process . The finish line is the certificate of occupancy .

Power, access, and rural utilities

On a desert lot, the power line is rarely sitting at your property edge waiting to be tapped. Running electricity to the house, plus building a driveway and access, adds both cost and weeks to the schedule before framing can start. So the power run, the meter spot, and a graded access road belong at the front of your calendar, right next to the well and septic. See how much it costs to extend power or utilities to a rural Arizona lot , how temporary construction power and the meter spot work , and whether you need a will-serve letter to build in Arizona .

Buying the lot and starting the build

Do your homework on the lot before you sign for it, not after. Check the zoning, the water source, the slope, and any HOA design rules first, because those four things, plus the rural setbacks and the 24-foot cap, decide how much house you can build and what it costs. Make sure you know whether the parcel is inside the town or out in the unincorporated county next door, since that sets the code. Then, before the first footing pours, the lot usually needs a grading or earthwork permit, a dust-control permit, and a drainage clearance, and you call 811 to have utilities marked before anyone digs. See how to buy land to build a house in Arizona , how to know if your lot is in the city or unincorporated county , grading or earthwork permit and SWPPP , dust-control permit , 811 Blue Stake before digging , how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC , and construction-to-permanent loan in Arizona .

Common Carefree questions

Two questions come up more than any others out here: which codes apply, and whether you can add a guest casita. Start with what building codes apply in Carefree for the plain-language code answer, and whether Cave Creek, Fountain Hills, and Carefree allow casitas and ADUs for accessory dwellings. For the whole project start to finish, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona and how long it takes to build a custom home in Arizona .

Sources

The framing facts in this guide come from the Town of Carefree Building Department and the codified Carefree Town Code Section 10-1-2 . Septic requirements are published by Maricopa County Onsite Wastewater , and Arizona's broader code-adoption picture is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona .

This guide is general information, not a code or zoning interpretation. Fees, editions, zoning rules, and HOA design guidelines change, so confirm the current requirements for your parcel with the Town of Carefree and your community's design committee before you rely on them.

title: Building a Custom Home in Casa Grande: The Complete Local Guide
summary: Building a custom home in Casa Grande means designing to the city's 2018 I-codes and 2017 NEC, paying a permit priced off the ICC valuation table plus a 65 percent plan review fee, and budgeting separate impact fees due before the permit issues. Expect about $225 to $400 per square foot in 2026, lower than north Scottsdale because Pinal County land costs less. The cheaper lot is the main reason the all-in number drops.
bodyHtml:
Two families drive down to look at the same house on paper. One builds it in north Scottsdale, one builds it in Casa Grande, and the Casa Grande family pays less. The framing, the drywall, the roof: all about the same. The land is what moves the number. Cheaper dirt down here is the single biggest reason the all-in cost drops, and it is the thread that runs through this whole guide.

There is one wrinkle worth knowing up front. Casa Grande is an incorporated city, so the city handles your permit and your inspections. But the moment you buy rural land at the city's edge, water and septic approvals swing over to Pinal County. Where your lot actually sits decides who you talk to.

This guide is the Casa Grande hub. Each section gives you the short answer, then links the deep page where you can confirm the rules and numbers for your exact lot.

What this guide covers

  • Cost to build in Casa Grande
  • Adopted building codes
  • Permit fees and the ICC valuation table
  • Development impact fees
  • Single-family zoning and setbacks
  • Well and septic on a rural Pinal County lot
  • Designing for heat, soil, and monsoon
  • Inspections and certificate of occupancy
  • Power, access, and rural utilities
  • Builder, financing, and the construction loan
  • Buying the lot and starting the build
  • Common Casa Grande questions

Cost to build in Casa Grande

Plan on roughly $225 to $400 per square foot to build here in 2026. Crews charge about the same wage whether they are pouring a slab in Casa Grande or in Scottsdale, and a bag of concrete costs what it costs. So when the final price comes in lower down here, it is almost always the lot doing the talking, not some secret discount on the house itself. Just remember the per-foot figure is only the build. On top of it sits a city permit, a 65 percent plan review fee, and separate impact fees. For the full breakdown, see how much it costs to build a custom home in Casa Grande and, for the metro baseline, cost to build a house per square foot in Arizona .

Adopted building codes (2018 I-codes)

Codes come in editions, like software versions, and Casa Grande is running the 2018 IRC along with the rest of the 2018 I-code family and the 2017 National Electrical Code. The city adopted them by reference in Title 15 of the City Code, with local tweaks put on the record by Resolution No. 5170. That puts Casa Grande on the same page as much of Pinal County and the outer metro, and one step behind Phoenix, which is on the 2024 codes. Here is why that matters to you: a plan drawn to satisfy a 2024 Phoenix reviewer can call for different framing, wiring, or energy details than a 2018 lot needs. Borrowing a set from a Phoenix build does not mean it passes here.

So the very first thing to nail down on any parcel near the city edge is whether it falls inside Casa Grande or in unincorporated Pinal County. That one answer sets your code and your permit office. For the full code list and the local edits, see Casa Grande adopted building codes and Arizona building code editions by city .

Permit fees and the ICC valuation table

Some cities charge permit fees by the square foot. Casa Grande does not. It prices your permit off the home's construction valuation, reading from the latest ICC Building Valuation Table on a tiered scale, so a bigger, pricier home carries a bigger permit. Plan review then adds 65 percent of the permit fee on top, plus a 5 percent technology recovery fee. The table gets refreshed now and then, and the posted numbers can trail behind, so pull the live fee before you lock your budget. See Casa Grande building permit and impact fees and, for how valuation pricing compares to per-foot cities, Arizona building permit fees: valuation vs per square foot . For the metro-wide picture, see building permit cost in Arizona .

Development impact fees

Here is a bill that surprises a lot of first-time builders. On top of the permit, Casa Grande collects development impact fees, and they come due before the permit ever issues. Think of them as your home's share of the growth it creates. Arizona law, A.R.S. 9-463.05, caps these fees to the actual cost of growth-related infrastructure, so the money goes to roads, parks, and public safety capacity that new homes lean on, not into general city spending. The same law makes the city show its work and publish the math, which is why the amounts live in the Infrastructure Improvements Plan and get updated on a schedule. That also means the figure moves. A number you jotted down a year ago may already be stale. Check the current amount before you finalize anything, because the impact fee and the valuation permit fee stacked together can add several thousand dollars over the per-foot build. See Casa Grande building permit and impact fees and the impact fees glossary entry .

Single-family zoning and setbacks

Your lot lines are not your building lines. Setbacks carve a smaller box inside them, and that box is where your house actually goes. In Casa Grande's R-1 single-family zone, you need a 7,000 square foot lot, a 15-foot front yard, a 20-foot setback for a street-facing garage, a 5 to 10-foot side yard, and a 20-foot rear yard. Larger rural zones push those numbers deeper. Height tops out at 28 feet across the residential zones. Learn your district before you design, and you design to the real buildable box instead of getting a nasty correction later.

The one that trips people up is that 20-foot garage setback. It shoves the front of the house back farther than the 15-foot front yard alone would ever suggest, and it can quietly eat into your driveway plans. For every district's exact yards, coverage, and height, see Casa Grande residential zoning and setbacks . For corner parcels, see corner lot and double frontage setbacks in Arizona , and for the general ceiling, how tall a custom home can be in Arizona .

Well and septic on a rural Pinal County lot

Buy a rural lot with no city utilities and you become your own water and sewer department. That means two separate approvals. First, a septic permit from the Pinal County Aquifer Protection Division, which ADEQ hands off to the county. Second, a private well, which the state authorizes through an ADWR Notice of Intent to Drill. On lots of 5 acres or fewer, the county reviews the well. The septic side kicks off with a soils evaluation and a perc test to see how fast the ground drains. The well side kicks off with that Notice of Intent. Neither one waits on the other, and both take time, so get them moving early.

Inside the city it is a different story. Many lots have municipal water and sewer sitting right at the street, and the whole well-and-septic dance disappears. Out on rural land, though, water and wastewater are the two things most likely to blow up your schedule, because neither one goes through the city. See Pinal County septic and well requirements , whether you need a soil test to build a house in Arizona , and how close a well can be to a septic system .

Designing for heat, soil, and monsoon

Before you fall in love with a floor plan, three desert forces already have a say in your house: heat, expansive soil, and monsoon storms. They shape every build across Pinal and Maricopa County, and Casa Grande is no exception. Weeks of 110-plus degree afternoons push you toward deep roof overhangs, low-gain glass, heavy insulation, and a high-SEER heat pump sized for the real load. A lot of ground out here swells when it gets wet and shrinks when it dries, and that movement will crack a slab that was not built for it. A soils report plus an engineered foundation, often a post-tensioned slab, keeps the concrete steady while the dirt moves underneath. Then the monsoon shows up fast in July and August, so your site has to shed that water away from the slab, not toward it.

These forces shape the bones of the house more than the layout does. And a soils report is what tells you which foundation your lot needs before you can price the build honestly. See whether you need a soil test to build a house in Arizona and the expansive soil glossary entry .

Inspections and certificate of occupancy

Your house gets checked in stages, not all at once at the end. Casa Grande inspects from foundation through final, and each stage has to pass before the next one buries it. Miss a rough-in inspection and you may be opening walls back up. Once the final inspection clears, the city hands over a Certificate of Occupancy, the piece of paper that says you can move in. On a rural well-and-septic lot, the list grows: the septic system and the water setup get their own sign-off from the county, apart from the city's building inspections. The permit submittals and the field process are in Casa Grande residential building permit requirements and the Casa Grande inspections process . The finish line is the certificate of occupancy .

Power, access, and rural utilities

Picture a bare lot with a dirt track leading in and no power pole in sight. That is a lot of rural parcels outside the city core, and it means the power run and the driveway are on you before the house can even start. Running electricity to the site, setting a meter spot, and grading an access road are all early-schedule work, right alongside the well and septic. Skip them in your planning and they will find you later, usually as delay. See how much it costs to extend power or utilities to a rural Arizona lot , how temporary construction power and the meter spot work , and whether you need a will-serve letter to build in Arizona .

Buying the lot and starting the build

The best time to catch a lot problem is before you own the lot. So before you sign, confirm three things: the zoning, the water source, and the utility access. Together they set both how much house you can build and what it will cost. Part of that check is settling whether the parcel sits inside the city or in unincorporated Pinal County, which decides your code and your permit office. Then, before a footing ever pours, the lot usually needs a grading or earthwork permit and a dust-control permit, and you call 811 to have buried lines marked before anyone digs. See how to buy land to build a house in Arizona , how to know if your lot is in the city or unincorporated county , grading or earthwork permit and SWPPP , dust-control permit , 811 Blue Stake before digging , how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC , and construction-to-permanent loan in Arizona .

Builder, financing, and the construction loan

Line up your builder and your money before the design locks, not after. The lender's appraisal and draw schedule end up steering both the budget and the calendar, so they need a seat at the table early. Check any builder against the Arizona Registrar of Contractors before you sign, because that ROC license is what stands behind the implied warranty on your home. Most people fund a build like this with a construction-to-permanent loan: it pays out in draws as the work gets done, then rolls into a normal mortgage when you finish, so you close once instead of twice. See how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC and the construction-to-permanent loan in Arizona .

Common Casa Grande questions

Most people who call us want to know three things: what it costs per foot, how the permit math works, and how long the whole thing takes. Start with how much it costs to build a custom home in Casa Grande and building permit cost in Arizona . For the whole project arc, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona and how long it takes to build a custom home in Arizona .

Sources

The framing facts in this guide come from the City of Casa Grande: the City Fee Schedule and Building Inspections . The cap on development fees is set in Arizona Revised Statutes 9-463.05 , and Arizona's broader code-adoption picture is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona .

This guide is general information, not a code or zoning interpretation. Fees, editions, and zoning rules change, so confirm the current requirements for your parcel with the City of Casa Grande and Pinal County before you rely on them.

Sources

  • City of Casa Grande City Fee Schedule
  • City of Casa Grande Building Inspections
  • Arizona Revised Statutes 9-463.05 (Development fees)
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona

title: Building a Custom Home in Cave Creek: The Complete Local Guide
summary: Building a custom home in Cave Creek means designing to the town's new 2024 I-codes, putting in a private well or hauled water and a septic system on most lots, and fitting a home inside the town's deep-yard Desert Rural zoning. Expect about $325 to $650 per square foot in 2026, with desert estates passing $800. The lot, not the finish, drives the cost.
bodyHtml:
Out here, the land does most of the talking. A flat city lot hands you water at the curb and a sewer line under the street. A Cave Creek lot hands you raw desert, and you build the water, the wastewater, and often the road yourself. That is the real difference, and it shows up long before you pick a single finish. A build typically runs $325 to $650 per square foot in 2026, with high-end desert estates passing $800. But the per-foot finish number is not what drives the budget here. The 2024 family of International Codes the town now runs, the well or hauled water, the septic system, the desert and hillside site work, and the town's deep Desert Rural yards are what set a Cave Creek home apart.

Think of this page as your Cave Creek map. Each section gives you the short answer first, then points you to the deep page where you can confirm the rules and numbers for your exact lot. One thing to settle up front: Cave Creek runs newer codes than the unincorporated county right next door, so which side of the town line your lot sits on changes the rules that apply.

What this guide covers

  • Cost to build in Cave Creek
  • Adopted building codes
  • Permit and impact fees
  • Desert Rural zoning and setbacks
  • Well and septic on a Cave Creek lot
  • Desert and hillside site work
  • Inspections and certificate of occupancy
  • Power, access, and rural utilities
  • Buying the lot and starting the build
  • Site prep, builder, and financing
  • Common Cave Creek questions

Cost to build in Cave Creek

Two neighbors can build the same house and pay very different totals, and the reason usually has nothing to do with the kitchen. Plan on $325 to $650 per square foot in 2026, with high-end desert estates passing $800. (volatile:true, confirm closer.) That range covers the house. What it does not cover is the desert underneath it: the well or hauled water, the septic system, the grading on a sloped or rocky lot, and the long driveways the town's deep yards force. Those pieces are where budgets swing. For the full breakdown, see how much it costs to build a custom home in Cave Creek .

Adopted building codes (2024 I-codes)

Cave Creek recently moved to a newer rulebook, and the date matters. As of January 1, 2026, the town builds to the 2024 IRC and most of the 2024 I-code family, plus the 2023 National Electrical Code and a few 2025-edition codes, all adopted by Resolution R2025-01. There is one break in your favor: for homes, the town stuck with the 2018 residential energy code instead of the 2024 version, which keeps the energy paperwork lighter. The catch is that the county surrounding the town has not moved. It still runs the 2018 codes and the 2017 NEC. So a plan drawn for a rural county lot does not automatically pass in town.

Here is where people get burned. A single road can be the town line, and lots along the edge sit just inside or just outside it. Cross that line and the code changes, the permit counter changes, and the inspectors change. A set drawn to the county's 2018 IRC and 2017 NEC will not clear the town's 2024 codes and 2023 NEC, and a town set will not fit a county lot either. That makes one question the very first thing to answer on any Cave Creek parcel: which jurisdiction am I actually in? Everything downstream rides on the answer. For the full code list and the per-code amendments, see Cave Creek adopted building codes and Arizona building code editions by city .

Permit and impact fees

Cave Creek does not charge a flat fee for your permit. It prices the permit off what the town figures your home is worth to build, its construction valuation, using the ICC Building Valuation Table set in Town Code Section 151.14. Plan review is then a set minimum or a percentage of that permit fee, whichever comes out higher. The valuation table the town posts online can lag behind the real one, so pull the live number before you lock a budget. (volatile:true, confirm closer.) See Cave Creek building permit and impact fees and, for how valuation pricing stacks up against per-foot cities, Arizona building permit fees: valuation vs per square foot .

Desert Rural zoning and setbacks

Cave Creek likes its homes with room to breathe, and the Desert Rural (DR) zones enforce it. DR-190, DR-89, and DR-70 each ask for a 60 foot front and rear yard and a 30 foot side yard, and they cap the building at 10 percent lot coverage. DR-43 eases up a little: 40 foot front and rear, 20 foot sides, 15 percent coverage, with height topping out at 25 feet. Deep yards and low coverage caps are the reason Cave Creek houses sit so far back on such big desert lots.

The number that catches buyers off guard is that 10 percent coverage cap in the larger districts. On a full acre, 10 percent leaves you only a few thousand square feet under roof. Want a bigger house? You go up a second story, or you stretch a single level out inside those deep setbacks. And a 60 foot front yard is not just a lawn. It is a long driveway to build and grade, which is more site work than any city lot. Learn your district before you draw a floor plan and you will know your real buildable box, not just where the lot lines fall. For every district's exact yards, coverage, and height, see Cave Creek residential zoning and setbacks . For corner parcels, see corner lot and double frontage setbacks in Arizona .

Well and septic on a Cave Creek lot

Turn on a faucet in a new Cave Creek home and the water has to come from somewhere you paid to bring there. Most custom lots have no town sewer or water, so the house needs a septic system permitted by Maricopa County Environmental Services, plus either a private well authorized by the Arizona Department of Water Resources or a hauled-water setup with tanks on site. The septic side opens with a soils evaluation and a perc test. The well side opens with a Notice of Intent to Drill filed with ADWR. Each runs on its own clock, which is exactly why they belong early in the schedule, not late.

These two approvals slip more Cave Creek schedules than anything else, because neither one runs through the town. A perc test can fail outright, or pass but demand a bigger, pricier septic field on tight desert soil. A well can come in weak or dry, and suddenly your whole water plan changes. Depth drives the drilling bill, so the lot next door can cost thousands more or less than yours. That uncertainty is why smart builders confirm the water and the soil before they finalize the house, since a bad result can shove the building pad to a new spot. See Maricopa County septic and well requirements and whether you need a soil test to build a house in Arizona .

Desert and hillside site work

Cave Creek land is desert, and a lot of it slopes. Before the house even starts, that ground has to be graded, drained, retained, and reached, and each of those costs money. A hillside parcel needs engineered foundations and cut-and-fill work a flat lot never touches, and the desert drainage rules bend your site plan to fit. Then the town's deep yards layer on top of the terrain to decide where the house is even allowed to sit. The dollar impact shows up in how much it costs to build in Cave Creek , and the buildable-area limits come from Cave Creek residential zoning and setbacks .

Inspections and certificate of occupancy

The town checks the work in stages, from foundation all the way through final, and each stage has to pass before anyone buries it behind drywall or dirt. Clear the final inspection and the town hands you a Certificate of Occupancy. On a well-and-septic lot, add two more items to the list: the septic system and the water setup, which the county signs off on its own, apart from the town's building inspections. Line the stages up so the right approval is already in hand before each cover-up. Miss one and you may be tearing finished work back open to prove it was done right. The permit submittals and the field process live in Cave Creek residential building permit requirements and the Cave Creek inspections process . The finish line is the certificate of occupancy .

Power, access, and rural utilities

On a Cave Creek desert lot, the power line is rarely waiting at your property corner. Getting electricity to the house and getting yourself to the house, a real driveway, both take money and time before the build can move. Power runs, a meter spot, and a graded access road are early-schedule work on a rural parcel, right alongside the well and septic. See how much it costs to extend power or utilities to a rural Arizona lot , how temporary construction power and the meter spot work , and whether you need a will-serve letter to build in Arizona .

Buying the lot and starting the build

The best money you spend on a Cave Creek home gets spent before you own the land. Check the lot's zoning, its water source, and its slope first, because the Desert Rural setbacks, the well-or-haul-water question, and the terrain together decide how much house you can build and what it will cost. Pin down one more thing while you are at it: whether the parcel sits inside the town or out in the unincorporated county, because that is what sets the code. See how to buy land to build a house in Arizona , how to know if your lot is in the city or unincorporated county , and the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Site prep, builder, and financing

Long before a footing pours, a Cave Creek lot usually needs a grading or earthwork permit, a dust-control permit, and a drainage clearance, and you make the 811 call before any shovel goes in the ground. Two more moves belong up front: hire a licensed builder, and lock down your financing before the design is final. See grading or earthwork permit and SWPPP , dust-control permit , 811 Blue Stake before digging , how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC , and construction-to-permanent loan in Arizona .

Common Cave Creek questions

Two topics come up more than any others out here: which codes apply, and whether you can add a casita. Start with what building codes apply in Cave Creek for the plain-language answer on codes, and whether Cave Creek, Fountain Hills, and Carefree allow casitas and ADUs for accessory dwellings. To see the whole project start to finish, read the steps to building a custom home in Arizona and how long it takes to build a custom home in Arizona .

Septic requirements are published by Maricopa County Onsite Wastewater , and Arizona's broader code-adoption picture is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona .

This guide is general information, not a code or zoning interpretation. Fees, editions, and zoning rules change, so confirm the current requirements for your parcel with the Town of Cave Creek before you rely on them.

title: Building a Custom Home in Fountain Hills: The Complete Local Guide
summary: Building a custom home in Fountain Hills means designing to the town's 2024 I-codes, installing a required fire-sprinkler system, respecting hillside disturbance limits on sloped lots, and budgeting the town's development impact fee. Expect about $325 to $650 per square foot in 2026, with hillside estates passing $800. Sprinklers, slope, and the impact fee set it apart from neighboring cities.
bodyHtml:
Cross the town line into Fountain Hills and the rulebook gets thicker. This is one of the few towns in the Valley that makes you sprinkler the whole house, watches how much of a sloped lot you are allowed to touch, and charges its own impact fee on top. It also builds to the newest 2024 family of International Codes. Put it all together and a home here usually runs $325 to $650 per square foot in 2026, with the hillside estates climbing past $800.

Three of those costs are the reason building in Fountain Hills prices higher than in the town next door: the required sprinklers, the hillside limits, and the impact fee most neighbors never charge. This guide is your Fountain Hills hub. Each section hands you the short answer, then points to the deep page where you can pin down the rules and numbers for your exact lot. One thing to keep in mind: the town jumped to the 2024 codes in 2025, so its rules sit a step ahead of older sets and of nearby areas still running the 2018 cycle.

What this guide covers

  • Cost to build in Fountain Hills
  • Adopted building codes
  • Required fire sprinklers
  • Permit and impact fees
  • Zoning and setbacks
  • Hillside and disturbance limits
  • Inspections and certificate of occupancy
  • Community design guidelines (FireRock)
  • Buying the lot and starting the build
  • Site prep: grading, dust, and drainage
  • Choosing a builder and financing
  • Common Fountain Hills questions

Cost to build in Fountain Hills

Plan on $325 to $650 per square foot to build here in 2026, and past $800 for a hillside estate. (volatile:true, confirm closer.) Why the spread, and why higher than the flat-lot town down the road? Three costs that come with the address: the fire-sprinkler system every home needs, the disturbance limits on a sloped lot, and a town impact fee most nearby cities never charge.

None of these are the kind of thing you can trim later when the budget gets tight. The sprinklers are a full design-and-install job. The hillside work means an engineered foundation and grading a flat lot would never need. The impact fee is a flat charge per home, set by town resolution, and it does not care how you finish the kitchen. That is the split worth understanding early: the per-foot finish number is yours to control, but these three are handed to you by the town and the lot. For the full breakdown, current impact-fee figure included, see how much it costs to build a custom home in Fountain Hills .

Adopted building codes (2024 I-codes)

The code your home has to meet depends on a single date: when the town receives your permit application. Turn it in on or after September 1, 2025, and you build to the 2024 IRC and the rest of the 2024 I-code family, plus the 2023 National Electrical Code, all adopted by Ordinance 25-06 with local tweaks. Energy runs on the tighter 2024 IECC as amended. Here is the trap: Fountain Hills is a step ahead of areas still on the 2018 cycle, so a plan set drawn to an older code is not automatically legal on a town lot. Match the plans to the edition tied to your application date. For the full code list and amendments, see Fountain Hills adopted building codes and Arizona building code editions by city .

Required fire sprinklers

Almost every new home in Fountain Hills needs an automatic fire-sprinkler system, and not just over the couch. The lines run through every level and out into the garage and carport too. Most neighboring cities ask for nothing of the sort, so this is a cost a Fountain Hills build carries alone, and it hits your mechanical and water budget from the very first estimate.

The reach is what surprises people. Because it covers the garage and carport on top of the living space, the system gets designed, reviewed, and inspected as its own separate scope, with its own review fee. And if your lot draws from hauled or well water instead of a town main, the supply and storage have to keep up with the sprinkler demand, which can push you into a bigger tank or a stronger pump. There is a bit of history behind why the rule sticks: Fountain Hills adopted it in 2008, one year before a 2009 Arizona statute barred cities from newly requiring home sprinklers. The town got in under the wire, so its mandate stays while newer cities cannot add one. Budget the system and its review from day one. See Fountain Hills fire-sprinkler requirements and the plain-language answer in do I need fire sprinklers in a new home in Fountain Hills .

Permit and impact fees

Three separate charges land on a new home here: the building permit and plan-review fee, a separate fire-sprinkler review fee, and a development impact fee. That last one, set by town resolution, is the big reason a build here costs more than in a neighbor like Paradise Valley, which charges no impact fee at all. The catch is that the fee is a moving target. The resolutions get updated and carry effective dates and freeze windows, so nail down the live number before you build it into your budget. (volatile:true, confirm closer.) See Fountain Hills building permit and impact fees and, for how permit pricing works across the metro, Arizona building permit fees: valuation vs per square foot .

Zoning and setbacks

Before you sketch a single wall, your zoning district tells you how much of the lot you can actually build on. Fountain Hills single-family zoning spans from R1-6 on the small lots up to R1-190 on the one-acre-plus parcels. A R1-43 lot, for example, wants a 40 foot front yard, 30 foot sides, a 40 foot rear yard, and holds you to 20 percent lot coverage. Drop to a smaller R1-18 lot and the yards shrink to 20, 10, and 30 feet, all under a 30 foot height cap. That footprint is your starting point, and any hillside rule only shrinks it from there. For every district's exact yards, coverage, and height, see Fountain Hills residential zoning and setbacks . For corner parcels, see corner lot and double frontage setbacks in Arizona .

Hillside and disturbance limits

A lot of Fountain Hills sits on a hillside, and the steeper your ground, the less of it the town lets you touch. The rule caps how much of a sloped parcel you can disturb, and it tightens as the grade climbs, so on the steepest lots you can clear and grade the least. Slope also brings work a flat lot never asks for: an engineered foundation, retaining walls, and cut-and-fill. All of that costs money and dictates where on the lot the house can even go. So the slope shapes two things at once, your site plan and your budget. The buildable-area limits flow from Fountain Hills residential zoning and setbacks , and the cost effect is detailed in how much it costs to build in Fountain Hills .

Inspections and certificate of occupancy

The town checks your work in stages, from the foundation all the way to the final, and nothing gets covered up until that stage passes. Because Fountain Hills mandates sprinklers, the system earns its own rough and final checks, which makes the inspection list here one step longer than in a town without the requirement. The move that keeps you on schedule is simple: line up the sprinkler inspections next to the framing and final stages so they do not stall everything behind them. When the final passes, the town hands you a Certificate of Occupancy, the paper that says you can move in. The permit submittals and the field process are in Fountain Hills residential building permit requirements and the Fountain Hills inspections process . The end point is the certificate of occupancy .

Community design guidelines (FireRock)

In a gated community like FireRock, the town is not the first hurdle. An architectural committee is, and it signs off on your design before the town ever sees it. Its rules run stricter than the town code, so this is the tougher gate of the two. Over in FireRock Ridge, homes have to be single-story or split-level, no wall or roofline can rise above 20 feet over natural grade, roof slope tops out at 5/12, paint has to sit at 50 percent LRV or lower, and the home has to hit at least 2,500 square feet of livable area. Design to those numbers from the first sketch, because reworking a plan to satisfy the committee later is slow and expensive. See FireRock Fountain Hills design guidelines , and for the broader luxury-community color rule, LRV paint color rules in north Scottsdale luxury communities .

Buying the lot and starting the build

Do this before you sign for the lot, not after. Check the zoning, the slope, and any community design rules first, because the district setbacks, the hillside disturbance limits, and a committee like FireRock decide your buildable area before design even begins. Two lots the same size can hand you two very different homes: a flat R1-18 parcel and a steep hillside R1-43 parcel are not the same canvas. See how to buy land to build a house in Arizona , the steps to building a custom home in Arizona , and what happens during the design phase of a custom home .

Site prep: grading, dust, and drainage

The dirt comes before the concrete, and it comes with its own stack of permits. Before a footing pours on a Fountain Hills lot, you usually need a grading or earthwork permit, a dust-control permit, and a drainage clearance, and a hillside lot piles engineered cut-and-fill on top. One rule is not optional: call 811 before you dig. See grading or earthwork permit and SWPPP , dust-control permit , drainage clearance and the Maricopa County grading matrix , and 811 Blue Stake before digging . Plan temporary construction power too.

Choosing a builder and financing

Lock in your builder and your financing before the design is final, and make sure the budget already carries the sprinkler system and impact fee a Fountain Hills build tacks on. Then check the builder is who they say they are: verify the license through the Arizona Registrar of Contractors (ROC) . See how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC , what questions to ask a custom home builder before hiring , how to compare custom home builder bids in Arizona , and construction-to-permanent loan in Arizona .

Common Fountain Hills questions

The questions we hear most about Fountain Hills circle three things: codes, sprinklers, and backyard dwellings. Start with what building codes apply in Fountain Hills , then do I need fire sprinklers in a new home in Fountain Hills . On accessory dwellings, see whether Cave Creek, Fountain Hills, and Carefree allow casitas and ADUs . To see the whole project from start to finish, read the steps to building a custom home in Arizona and how long it takes to build a custom home in Arizona .

Sources

The framing facts in this guide come from the Town of Fountain Hills: Adopted Codes , Town Code Chapter 7 (Buildings and Building Regulations) , and the Zoning Ordinance, Section 10.09 . Arizona's broader code-adoption picture is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona .

This guide is general information, not a code or zoning interpretation. Fees, editions, and hillside rules change, so confirm the current requirements for your parcel with the Town of Fountain Hills Building Safety Division before you rely on them.
