title: Building a Custom Home in Paradise Valley: The Complete Local Guide
summary: Building a custom home in Paradise Valley means designing to the town's older 2015 I-codes, fitting an estate on a one-acre minimum R-43 lot, staying under a 25 percent floor-area cap, and clearing hillside review on sloped ground. Expect $500 to $1,000-plus per square foot in 2026, the highest range in metro Phoenix. Lot size and site rules, not finish, set the all-in cost.
bodyHtml:
Most Arizona towns want density. Paradise Valley wants breathing room, and it wrote that wish into law. You get a full acre per home at a minimum, a rule that says your house can only cover about a quarter of the lot, and a building code the town froze back in 2016 and has left alone since. On sloped ground, a Hillside Building Committee weighs in before you can build at all. Put those pieces together and you understand why a home here can run anywhere from $500 to well past $1,000 per square foot in 2026, the priciest range anywhere in metro Phoenix. And notice what is missing from that list: fancy finishes. The land and the site rules do most of the pushing on the final number, long before you choose a countertop.

Treat this guide as your Paradise Valley home base. Each section gives you the short answer, then links to a deeper page where you can check the exact rules and numbers for your own lot. One caution up front: this town plays by unusually old code, so a plan drawn for a neighboring city almost never drops in cleanly.

What this guide covers

  • Cost to build in Paradise Valley
  • Adopted building codes
  • Permit and impact fees
  • Zoning, setbacks, and the one-acre minimum
  • Floor-area ratio and lot coverage
  • Hillside development review
  • Well and septic (no town utilities)
  • Inspections and certificate of occupancy
  • Buying the lot and starting the build
  • Site prep: grading, dust, and drainage
  • Choosing a builder and financing
  • Common Paradise Valley questions

Cost to build in Paradise Valley

Budget somewhere between $500 and $1,000-plus per square foot to build here in 2026, the top of the range for metro Phoenix. (volatile:true, confirm closer.) People assume that number is all about finish level. It is not. Four things pile onto the total: the one-acre minimum lot, the rule capping your house at a quarter of the lot, the hillside review, and the fact that many lots have no town water or sewer, so you build your own. Each one adds cost before the first tile is chosen. For the full cost breakdown, see how much it costs to build a custom home in Paradise Valley .

Adopted building codes (2015 I-codes)

Here is the quirk that trips up more plans than anything else in town. Paradise Valley builds to the 2015 IRC and the rest of the 2015 I-code family, plus the 2014 National Electrical Code, with one exception: the 2021 International Fire Code sits on top. The town locked in the 2015 codes with Ordinance 2016-02 on May 26, 2016, and never moved off them. That is two to three editions behind a city like Phoenix. So a plan set drawn for a 2021 or 2024 lot can come back marked up here, even when the newer work is honestly better built.

An old code cycle cuts both ways. The upside is less paperwork. The 2015 energy code asks for less than a 2024 city's energy path, so proving compliance is simpler. The trap is reuse. A designer wraps up a Scottsdale or Phoenix job, then carries those same sheets over here, and now the electrical and energy details do not match what the town adopted. The plan bounces back for corrections. Watch the fire code most of all, because it runs a step ahead of everything else. Pull fire-access, sprinkler, and water-supply details to the 2021 IFC even while the rest of the set follows 2015. For the exact code list and the local design values, see Paradise Valley adopted building codes and Arizona building code editions by city .

Permit and impact fees

The town prices your building permit off construction valuation, meaning the estimated dollar value of the home, using the building valuation data in its Master Fee Schedule. In-house plan review then adds a percentage of that permit fee on top. Here is a nice surprise, though: Paradise Valley charges no municipal impact fee at all, so on that one line it comes in lighter than a neighbor like Fountain Hills. Valuation tables and review fees do shift, so pin down the current numbers before you budget. (volatile:true, confirm closer.) See Paradise Valley building permit and impact fees and, for how valuation pricing works, Arizona building permit fees: valuation vs per square foot .

Zoning, setbacks, and the one-acre minimum

Space is the whole point here, and the zoning proves it. The base R-43 district gives every home a full acre, with a 40-foot front and rear yard and 20-foot sides. Step up to the larger R-175 district and you need more than four acres, with 40-foot yards on every side. Height stays low too. Most lots top out near 24 feet, short of the 30 feet you would get in nearby cities, which is exactly why homes here stretch out across the lot instead of stacking up. For every district's exact yards, lot size, and height, see Paradise Valley residential zoning and setbacks . For corner parcels, see corner lot and double frontage setbacks in Arizona .

Floor-area ratio and lot coverage

Here is the rule that catches people who assumed a big lot meant a big house. Paradise Valley limits your total floor area to about 25 percent of the lot. That is the floor-area ratio, or FAR, and it holds even on a generous acre-plus parcel. On a one-acre lot, the FAR cap teams up with the setbacks and the height limit to fix your maximum house size before you sketch a thing. It is also why two different builders on matching lots land on nearly the same maximum footprint. The math forces it.

This cap is the reason the town still feels like an estate community and not a wall-to-wall subdivision. Run the numbers: a full acre is about 43,560 square feet, so a 25 percent cap points you toward a home in the low five figures of square footage, and that is before garages, covered patios, and other counted spaces start eating into it. Since the cap is a share of the lot, more land buys more house, which is a big part of why acreage drives the price so directly around here. Design to the cap from your very first sketch, because a plan that blows past it gets cut back in review, and that is a painful way to lose square footage. The setback and coverage figures that feed the FAR math are in Paradise Valley residential zoning and setbacks , and the cost effect of the cap is in how much it costs to build in Paradise Valley .

Hillside development review

Buy a lot on a slope and the town changes the deal: the steeper the ground, the less of it you are allowed to touch. That is the heart of the Hillside Development Regulations (Article XXII), which kick in on any land sloped 10 percent or more. Watch how fast the allowance shrinks. At a 10 percent slope you can disturb 60 percent of the building site. At 15 percent it falls to about 34 percent. On genuinely steep ground it drops into single digits. And every hillside home goes in front of a Hillside Building Committee before the town will issue a permit, so this is a real review, not a rubber stamp. See Paradise Valley hillside development requirements for the slope-to-disturbance table and the committee process.

Well and septic (no town utilities)

Plenty of Paradise Valley lots have no town sewer or water, and that surprises people. When yours does not, the approvals move off the town's desk and onto the county's and the state's. A septic system needs a permit from Maricopa County Environmental Services, which begins with a soils evaluation and a perc test. A private well needs the go-ahead from the Arizona Department of Water Resources. Both run on their own clocks, and those clocks can be slow, so start them early rather than treating them as a formality near the end. See Maricopa County septic and well requirements .

Inspections and certificate of occupancy

The town checks the home in stages as it rises, from the foundation to the final walkthrough, and each stage has to pass before the crew covers that work with the next. Clear the final inspection and the town issues a Certificate of Occupancy, the paper that makes the home legal to live in. The permit package and the required plan submittals are detailed in Paradise Valley residential building permit requirements and the Paradise Valley inspections process . The finish line is the certificate of occupancy .

Buying the lot and starting the build

Run the zoning, slope, and FAR math before you buy the lot, not after, because those three numbers set your maximum house before you draw a single line. The one-acre minimum, the 25 percent floor-area cap, and any hillside review together decide how much home the ground can hold. A flat R-43 acre and a steep hillside acre may cost the same, yet they give you two very different houses. Once the land checks out, the rest follows a familiar path: design, permitting, then construction, in that order. See how to buy land to build a house in Arizona , the steps to building a custom home in Arizona , and what happens during the design phase of a custom home .

Site prep: grading, dust, and drainage

Long before a footing pours, the lot itself has a to-do list. On most Paradise Valley parcels that means a grading or earthwork permit, a dust-control permit, and a drainage clearance, and a hillside lot piles on engineered cut-and-fill work. Think of this as its own phase, with its own permits, rather than a quick step before the real build. See grading or earthwork permit and SWPPP , dust-control permit , and drainage clearance and the Maricopa County grading matrix . Call 811 Blue Stake before digging , and plan temporary construction power .

Choosing a builder and financing

Settle your builder and your financing before the design locks, because both steer the timeline and, at Paradise Valley prices, the budget leaves little room for a stumble. The lender's appraisal and draw schedule set the pace, so you want them in place early. And before you shake hands with any builder, run the license through the Arizona Registrar of Contractors (ROC) . See how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC , what questions to ask a custom home builder before hiring , and how to compare custom home builder bids in Arizona . On financing, see construction-to-permanent loan in Arizona and construction loan requirements in Arizona .

Common Paradise Valley questions

Two topics come up more than any others: that old code cycle and the estate-lot rules. Start with the steps to building a custom home in Arizona for the full arc, then lean on the code, zoning, and hillside pages above for the town-specific detail. Since the 2015 codes trail the rest of the metro by so much, the smartest first move is simply confirming which edition applies to your permit, which we walk through in Paradise Valley adopted building codes . For a home built to shrug off the heat on an estate lot, see how to design a custom home to stay cool in the Arizona heat and how long it takes to build a custom home in Arizona .

Sources

The framing facts in this guide come from the Town of Paradise Valley: the Town Code, Chapter 5 (Building and Construction) , the Building Department , and the Hillside Building Committee . Arizona's broader code-adoption picture is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona .

This guide is general information, not a code or zoning interpretation. Fees, editions, FAR rules, and hillside limits change, so confirm the current requirements for your parcel with the Paradise Valley Building Department before you rely on them.

Sources

  • Town of Paradise Valley Town Code (Chapter 5, Building and Construction)
  • Town of Paradise Valley Building Department
  • Town of Paradise Valley Hillside Building Committee (Article XXII review)
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona

title: Building a Custom Home in Phoenix: The Complete Local Guide
summary: Building a custom home in Phoenix means designing to the 2024 Phoenix Building Construction Code, working a flat infill or teardown lot served by city water and sewer, and paying impact fees that depend on which service area your lot sits in. Expect about $275 to $550 per square foot in 2026, often less than north Scottsdale because lots cost less. Location, not the lot's terrain, is the big variable.
bodyHtml:
Two Phoenix lots a mile apart can carry very different price tags for the exact same house. The reason usually is not the dirt. It is which service area the lot lands in and what impact fees come with it. Most Phoenix sites are flat infill or teardown parcels already hooked to city water and sewer, which strips away the well-and-septic headaches rural buyers face. A build here runs about $275 to $550 per square foot in 2026, often cheaper than north Scottsdale because the land costs less and the ground is flat. But an infill lot in the built-out core can dodge most of the growth-area fees an edge subdivision pays, while a teardown adds demolition and, on an older house, asbestos work.

This guide is your Phoenix hub. Each section gives you the short answer first, then links the deep page where you can confirm the rules and numbers for your exact lot. Two facts run under all of it: Phoenix builds to newer codes than most of its smaller neighbors, and its impact-fee math shifts block to block. So where the parcel sits drives both the rules and the budget.

What this guide covers

  • Cost to build in Phoenix
  • Adopted building codes
  • Service-area impact fees and permits
  • Single-family zoning and setbacks
  • Infill, teardown, and demolition
  • City water, sewer, and the will-serve letter
  • Designing for Phoenix heat, soil, and monsoon
  • Inspections and certificate of occupancy
  • Casitas and accessory dwelling units
  • Builder, financing, and the construction loan
  • Buying the lot and starting the build
  • Common Phoenix questions

Cost to build in Phoenix

Two things keep Phoenix costs below north Scottsdale: cheaper land and flat lots that need almost no grading. Figure $275 to $550 per square foot to build in 2026. From there, location does the heavy lifting on your final number. A built-out infill lot skips most of the growth-area impact fees an edge subdivision has to pay, and a teardown tacks on the cost of demolishing the old house before the new one begins. For the full breakdown, see how much it costs to build a custom home in Phoenix and, for the metro baseline, cost to build a house per square foot in Arizona .

Adopted building codes (2024 PBCC)

Phoenix keeps its rulebook current. The city builds to the 2024 Phoenix Building Construction Code (PBCC), which pulls in the 2024 IRC, IBC, IPC, IMC, IFGC, and IECC plus the 2023 National Electrical Code, all with local amendments. The City Council signed off June 18, 2025, by Ordinance G-7397, and it took effect August 1, 2025. That leaves Phoenix a step ahead of most smaller neighbors still running the 2018 codes. The gap matters, because a 2024 detail and a 2018 detail can call for different framing, wiring, or energy specs on the very same house.

Since Phoenix runs a newer cycle than the towns around it, a plan set drawn for an older-code jurisdiction will not automatically clear here. So the first move on any Phoenix parcel is simple: make sure it is actually inside the city and not a pocket of unincorporated county, because that one fact decides which code applies. For the full code list and the local edits, see Phoenix adopted building codes and Arizona building code editions by city . For the plain-language version, see what building codes apply in Phoenix .

Service-area impact fees and permits

Here is where the map decides your bill. Phoenix charges new-home impact fees by service area, and most of them only hit designated growth areas, not the built-out core. In a growth area, a single-family home pays a fire fee of about $1,165 to $1,833, a police fee near $711 to $1,118, a $348 parks fee, and a $70 library fee, plus water and sewer fees, all set by Ordinance G-7375. Which service area your lot falls in sets the total, so two lots a few miles apart can owe very different amounts. Confirm the live numbers before you budget.

This is the single biggest reason a Phoenix build can pencil out cheaper than a comparable lot on the edge of the metro. An infill teardown in an area the city already serves often skips the growth fees altogether. Keep in mind the building permit fee is its own separate charge on top. For the full fee picture, see Phoenix building permit and impact fees , the impact fees glossary entry , and building permit cost in Arizona .

Single-family zoning and setbacks

Your zoning district quietly draws the outline of the house before you do. Phoenix single-family zoning runs from R1-6 on 6,000 square foot lots up to RE-43 on one-acre lots, and each district writes its own setbacks. R1-6 lets you sit 15 feet off the front and 10 off the side, at about 30 feet tall. RE-43 pushes that to a 40-foot front and 30-foot sides, also capped near 30 feet. Learn your district before you start designing and you learn the real box you build in, not just where the lot lines fall.

On a tight infill lot, the side yards and the lot-coverage limit usually pinch the footprint first. That is why so many Phoenix custom homes grow up instead of out. For every district's exact yards, coverage, and height, see Phoenix residential zoning and setbacks . For corner parcels, see corner lot and double frontage setbacks in Arizona , and for the general ceiling, how tall a custom home can be in Arizona .

Infill, teardown, and demolition

A lot of Phoenix custom homes start on land that already has a house on it. You build infill, on a single lot inside the built-out city, and often that means a teardown: the old house comes down before yours goes up. The trade is clear. You give up the easy blank lot and take on tighter setbacks and a demolition phase. In return you get mature trees, real streets, and a neighborhood that already knows itself. That is exactly why teardown-rebuild is so common in older Phoenix pockets like Arcadia and the Biltmore area, where the land is prized and the utilities are already at the curb.

The demolition is its own project with its own permit. On a pre-1980s house, an asbestos survey comes first, and it is not a formality. Popcorn ceilings, old floor tile, and dated insulation can all hide asbestos, and a licensed abatement contractor has to pull it out before a single wall comes down. Treat the survey and any abatement as an early, separate line in the budget, because it controls when demolition can even begin, and the cost swings with whatever the survey turns up. See how much asbestos abatement costs on an Arizona teardown or remodel , and for the earthwork side, whether you need a grading or earthwork permit and SWPPP and a dust-control permit .

City water, sewer, and the will-serve letter

This is one of the quiet perks of building in the city. A Phoenix infill lot almost always has city water and sewer waiting at the street, so the well and septic step that eats a rural schedule simply is not on your list. You still confirm service with a will-serve letter and pay the connection and impact fees. On an edge lot in a growth area, those water and sewer fees roll into the service-area total from earlier. See whether you need a will-serve letter to build in Arizona and how temporary construction power and the meter spot work .

Designing for Phoenix heat, soil, and monsoon

Before you fall in love with a floor plan, know that three things about Phoenix will shape the house whether you plan for them or not: heat, expansive soil, and monsoon storms. The 2024 energy code leans hard on all three. Weeks of 110-plus degree afternoons argue for deep roof overhangs, low-gain glass, spray-foam or thick batt insulation, and a high-SEER heat pump sized to the real load. Underneath, many Phoenix lots sit on expansive clay that swells when it is wet and shrinks when it dries. Left alone, that movement cracks a slab, so a soils report and an engineered foundation, often a post-tensioned slab, hold the house steady as the ground shifts. Then the monsoon hits fast in July and August, and your site grading has to carry that water away from the slab, not toward it.

Notice these three forces do more to define the structure than the layout ever will, and handling them up front is far cheaper than fixing them later. The soils report is the tell: it tells you which foundation the lot needs before you put a price on the build. See whether you need a soil test to build a house in Arizona and the expansive soil glossary entry .

Inspections and certificate of occupancy

Phoenix checks the house in stages, foundation to final, and every stage has to pass before the crew can close it up. When the final inspection clears, the city issues a Certificate of Occupancy, your green light to move in. The trick is sequencing: have the right approval in hand before each cover-up, because a missed sign-off can mean tearing finished work back open to prove it was built right. The permit submittals and the field process are in Phoenix residential building permit requirements and the Phoenix inspections process . The finish line is the certificate of occupancy .

Casitas and accessory dwelling units

Want a place for aging parents, grown kids, or a rental that helps cover the mortgage? Phoenix now allows a casita or accessory dwelling unit (ADU) on many single-family lots, a rule change that opened the door to guest quarters and rentals across the city. How big it can be, where it sits, and how much parking it needs all come down to your zoning district and your lot. See ADU and casita rules in Phoenix , ADU setback and size rules by city in Arizona , and whether you can build a guest house without a kitchen . For cost, see cost to build a casita in Arizona .

Buying the lot and starting the build

Answer three questions about the lot before you buy it, not after: what is the zoning, which service area is it in, and is there a house to tear down. Those three set how much home you can build and which impact fees land on you. Then double-check the parcel is inside the city and not a pocket of unincorporated county, since that picks the code. Before the first footing pours, the lot usually needs a grading or demolition permit, and you call 811 to get utilities marked before anyone digs. See how to buy land to build a house in Arizona , how to know if your lot is in the city or unincorporated county , 811 Blue Stake before digging , how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC , and construction-to-permanent loan in Arizona .

Builder, financing, and the construction loan

Line up your builder and your money before the design locks, because the lender's appraisal and draw schedule end up shaping both the budget and the calendar. Run any builder's license through the Arizona Registrar of Contractors before you sign, since that ROC license is what stands behind the implied warranty on your home. Most custom homes run on a construction-to-permanent loan that funds the build in draws, then flips to a regular mortgage when the house is done, so you close just once. See how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC and the construction-to-permanent loan in Arizona .

Common Phoenix questions

Three questions come up again and again in Phoenix: which codes apply, whether you can add an ADU, and how long the whole thing takes. Start with what building codes apply in Phoenix for the plain-language code answer and ADU and casita rules in Phoenix for accessory dwellings. For the whole project start to finish, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona and how long it takes to build a custom home in Arizona .

Sources

The framing facts in this guide come from the City of Phoenix: Building Construction Codes , the Impact Fees program page , and the Planning and Development Department . Arizona's broader code-adoption picture is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona .

This guide is general information, not a code or zoning interpretation. Fees, editions, and zoning rules change, so confirm the current requirements for your parcel with the City of Phoenix before you rely on them.

  • City of Phoenix Planning and Development Department
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona

title: Building a Custom Home in Rio Verde Foothills: The Complete Local Guide
summary: Building a custom home in Rio Verde Foothills means building on raw, unincorporated desert land under Maricopa County's 2018 codes, with no city water. You solve water with a private well or hauled water and an onsite storage tank, add a septic system and a power connection, and pull a county permit. Expect about $300 to $600 per square foot in 2026, plus heavy raw-land costs.
bodyHtml:
Turn a faucet on almost anywhere in the Phoenix metro and water shows up. Out in the Foothills, it does not. There is no city main under the road to tap into, and that one missing pipe reshapes the entire project. You will get water some other way, either by drilling a private well or trucking in hauled water to an onsite storage tank. Then you add a septic system, run power to the site, and pull your building permit through Maricopa County's 2018 codes, since no city has jurisdiction out here.

The build itself runs about $300 to $600 per square foot in 2026, but that figure is not where the drama lives. The land is. A raw parcel owes you nothing: water, septic, power, and access are all yours to build from scratch, and the water setup alone can run into the tens of thousands.

This guide is the Rio Verde Foothills hub. Each section gives you the short answer, then links the deep page where you can confirm the rules and numbers for your exact parcel. The county, not a city, reviews and inspects everything you build, and water is the question that decides how the rest of the project goes.

What this guide covers

  • Cost to build in Rio Verde Foothills
  • Raw land: what you need to build
  • Adopted building codes (county 2018)
  • Water: well, hauled water, and shared wells
  • Hauled-water cost and the EPCOR fill station
  • Financing and resale on a water-hauled home
  • Septic and well permits
  • A note on Trilogy at Verde River
  • Power and access to a raw lot
  • Site prep on county land
  • Choosing a builder and financing the build
  • Common Rio Verde questions

Cost to build in Rio Verde Foothills

Budget around $300 to $600 per square foot to build here in 2026, and then brace for the part of the budget that swings the hardest: the raw land. (volatile:true, confirm closer.) Getting water to the site, whether that is a drilled well or an EPCOR hauled-water hookup, can add tens of thousands before the framing crew ever shows up. Septic, power, and access pile on from there. Two lots that look identical from the road can land in very different places once you total it all. For the full breakdown of land, water, and finish costs, see how much it costs to build a custom home in Rio Verde .

Raw land: what you need to build

Here is the short list for building on rural land in Rio Verde: a private well or hauled water, a septic system sized by a soil test, a power connection, and a Maricopa County building permit. Because the Foothills sit in unincorporated county, the county is the one reviewing your plans against the 2018 building code and inspecting the work, not a city. Job one is proving your parcel truly falls in the unincorporated county and not just inside some nearby town line.

Building on raw land is a genuinely different animal than buying a finished lot in a subdivision. On a subdivision lot, the water tap, the sewer, and the power are already waiting at the curb. Out here, none of that exists until you make it exist and pay for it, right down to the graded driveway. That is why the land price is just the opening bid, and why two Rio Verde lots that look like twins can carry wildly different all-in costs once you know how deep the water sits, how the septic soils test, and how far the power has to travel. Line these site systems up early, because each one carries its own approval and its own wait, and a slow well or a failed perc test can freeze the whole build. For the full rural-land checklist, see what you need to build on rural land in Rio Verde .

Adopted building codes (county 2018)

No city, no city code. Since the Foothills are unincorporated, Maricopa County codes apply instead: the 2018 IRC, IBC, IPC, IMC, and IFGC, plus the 2017 National Electrical Code, all adopted by the Board of Supervisors on August 17, 2022. One county quirk actually works in your favor. Energy compliance out here is voluntary rather than mandatory, so you skip the energy paperwork a city like Scottsdale would demand. Flip side: a plan drawn to a neighboring city's newer 2021 or 2024 codes is not automatically good for a county lot, so do not assume a borrowed set clears review. See what building codes apply in Rio Verde Foothills and the full reference in Maricopa County adopted building codes .

Water: well, hauled water, and shared wells

You have three doors to get water into a Foothills home, and picking the right one shapes your cost, your schedule, and your loan. Most homes haul water, trucking it from the EPCOR fill station into an onsite storage tank of 2,500 gallons or more. Some drill a private exempt well, which the Arizona Department of Water Resources caps at a 35 gallons-per-minute pump. A few tie into a neighbor's well under a recorded shared agreement.

Make this call first, not last, because everything downstream leans on it. A drilled well costs more up front and carries a real risk of coming in low or dry, but it tends to finance and resell the most cleanly. Hauled water is cheaper to get going, yet it commits you to a permanent tank, deliveries that never stop, and lender limits that can knock out some loans entirely. A shared-well agreement only holds up if the recorded paperwork and the well's actual capacity can support one more home. Each option also lands in a different spot on your site plan, since a tank, a wellhead, and a shared line all want different places to sit. See how to get water to a home in Rio Verde Foothills and the framing in building in Rio Verde Foothills water rules .

Hauled-water cost and the EPCOR fill station

Hauled water is not a one-and-done hookup. It is a bill that shows up again and again. The EPCOR Rio Verde Foothills fill station is the go-to source, and just getting connected plus building the onsite storage tank runs into the tens of thousands, before you have paid for a single delivery. Those rates shift with demand, so price them against your actual build date, not some number from a couple years back. (volatile:true, confirm closer.) See how much it costs to haul water in Rio Verde Foothills and whether you can build without city water .

Financing and resale on a water-hauled home

Your water choice does not stop at the build budget. It follows the house to the closing table. FHA loans reject hauled-water cisterns on new construction, flat out, so tank homes usually get built and later sold with a well, a portfolio loan, or a cash buyer instead. A drilled well simply moves through lenders and buyers more smoothly than a hauled-water tank does, and that is worth sitting with before you commit to a water path. See whether a well or hauled water affects a Rio Verde property's value and financing .

Septic and well permits

Beyond the building permit, a Rio Verde home needs two more green lights, and they come from two different agencies. One is a septic permit from Maricopa County Environmental Services. The other is a private well authorized by ADWR. The septic approval opens with a soils evaluation and perc test that sizes the system to your ground. The well approval opens with a Notice of Intent to Drill. Each moves on its own clock, and both belong near the front of your schedule, not the back. See Maricopa County septic and well requirements .

A note on Trilogy at Verde River

Not every Rio Verde address means starting from bare dirt. Trilogy at Verde River is a master-planned, gated community in the area, and it comes with utilities, amenities, and its own design rules already in place. That is a completely different build than a custom home on open Foothills desert. Inside a community like Trilogy, you are working with community water, HOA design review, and platted utilities instead of hauling water and drilling a well. So figure out which Rio Verde you are actually buying into before you build a budget, because the water question that rules a raw-land build barely registers inside a planned community.

Power and access to a raw lot

Water is not the only thing missing from a raw Foothills lot. Most have no power at the property line and no real road in, either. That means running utilities and grading a driveway both add cost and both add waiting. A power run, a meter spot, and a graded access road are all early-schedule work, and some lots first need a will-serve letter from the utility confirming they will even hook you up. See how much it costs to extend power or utilities to a rural Arizona lot , how temporary construction power and the meter spot work , and what a will-serve letter is and whether you need one .

Site prep on county land

The ground has to get ready before the house does. Before a footing pours on a Rio Verde lot, the county usually wants a grading or earthwork permit and a dust-control permit, and you call 811 to get buried lines marked before digging. If your lot sits near a wash, add a floodplain use permit and a drainage clearance to the pile. None of these are instant, so give them room in the schedule. See grading or earthwork permit and SWPPP , dust-control permit , floodplain use permit near a wash , and 811 Blue Stake before digging .

Choosing a builder and financing the build

A builder who has only ever worked in subdivisions can get blindsided out here, so pick one with real rural experience, then line up financing built for a hauled-water or well lot. Lender rules on water source alone can make or break the loan. Run any contractor through the Arizona Registrar of Contractors (ROC) before you sign. See how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC , what questions to ask a custom home builder before hiring , construction loan requirements in Arizona , and whether you can finance a hauled-water or cistern home with a USDA or VA loan in Arizona .

Common Rio Verde questions

Almost every question we hear about Rio Verde traces back to the same two roots: water and the county code. Start with what you need to build on rural land in Rio Verde , then work through the water cluster: how to get water , building without city water , water rules , hauled-water cost , and financing and value . For the whole project arc, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona and how to buy land to build a house in Arizona .

Sources

The framing facts in this guide come from Maricopa County: Adopted Regulations and Onsite Wastewater (Septic) Forms and Applications . The hauled-water source is the EPCOR Rio Verde Foothills Water Fill Station , and Arizona's broader code-adoption picture is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona .

This guide is general information, not a code or water-rights interpretation. Codes, fees, and water rules change, so confirm the current requirements for your parcel with Maricopa County and ADWR before you rely on them.

Sources

  • Maricopa County Adopted Regulations (Local Additions and Addenda)
  • Maricopa County Onsite Wastewater (Septic) Forms and Applications
  • EPCOR: Rio Verde Foothills Water Fill Station (start service)
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona

title: Building a Custom Home in Scottsdale: The Complete Local Guide
summary: Building a custom home in Scottsdale means designing to the city's 2021 I-codes, clearing the Environmentally Sensitive Lands (ESL) ordinance on north and east desert lots, and meeting any community design review. Expect about $350 to $700 per square foot in 2026, with hillside lots and luxury north Scottsdale estates running higher. Land, not finish, is the real cost driver.
bodyHtml:
Buy a beautiful acre of north Scottsdale desert and you may find that only a slice of it is yours to build on. The mountains and the saguaros you paid for are, in large part, protected, and the city means to keep them that way. That single fact shapes almost everything about a custom home here, and it is why two identical-looking lots can produce two very different houses. Jematell Homes builds in Scottsdale, and this page pulls together every rule that steers a project through it. A build runs about $350 to $700 per square foot in 2026, and luxury north Scottsdale estates often pass $900. Yet the finish level is rarely the thing that decides the total. The land does. Hillside terrain, the desert-preservation rules, and a community's own design board are what push a Scottsdale build well past a flat-lot price.

Each section below gives you the short answer, then links the deep page where you can confirm the numbers and rules for your exact lot. Every plan we draw meets the city's current adopted code and overlay rules before a footing is poured.

What this guide covers

  • Cost to build in Scottsdale
  • Adopted building codes
  • Permit and impact fees
  • Zoning and setbacks
  • The ESL ordinance and desert preservation
  • Hillside and Foothills overlay lots
  • Inspections and certificate of occupancy
  • Community design guidelines (Troon North)
  • Buying the lot and starting the build
  • Site prep: grading, dust, and drainage
  • Choosing a builder and financing
  • Common Scottsdale questions

Cost to build in Scottsdale

Plan on about $350 to $700 per square foot in 2026, and expect north Scottsdale luxury estates to often pass $900. (volatile:true, confirm closer.) That per-foot number covers the house you can see. It does not cover the things that quietly run the bill up here: hillside site work, the open-space rules on desert lots, and a design review board's demands.

Land is the swing factor. A north Scottsdale view lot costs far more per acre than a flat south-city parcel, and a steep one then piles on extra engineering, retaining, and access costs before you have poured anything. The desert site work by itself, the plant salvage, the grading, the drainage, can reach six figures before the first stud goes up. One bit of good news: Scottsdale prices permits by the square foot, not by valuation, so your permit fees track house size instead of finish level. That makes them one of the few costs you can pin down early. For the full breakdown of land, site work, and finish costs, see how much it costs to build a custom home in Scottsdale .

Adopted building codes (2021 I-codes)

Scottsdale builds to the 2021 International Residential Code (IRC) and the rest of the 2021 I-code family, plus the 2020 National Electrical Code, with local City Council amendments on top. The building and fire codes took effect January 1, 2023. Here is the part people miss: the edition you build to is fixed by the day your permit issues, not by today's calendar. Hand plan review a set drawn to an older 2018 energy or wiring spec and it can come right back to you. For the exact code list, effective dates, and amendment documents, see Scottsdale adopted building codes , and the cross-city picture in Arizona building code editions by city .

Permit and impact fees

Most metro cities price a permit off what your home is worth to build. Scottsdale does not. It charges by the square foot, which is unusual around here and, for most custom homes, easier to predict. You pay a base fee, a per-square-foot charge on the air-conditioned area, a lower per-foot charge on covered space that is not air-conditioned, and a Certificate of Occupancy fee. Water and sewer development fees ride separately, billed by meter size. The city sets the actual rates in an annual fee schedule that changes every fiscal year, so pull the live numbers before you budget. (volatile:true, confirm closer.) See Scottsdale building permit and impact fees for the current schedule, and Arizona building permit fees: valuation vs per square foot for how Scottsdale's method compares.

Zoning and setbacks

Before any desert overlay touches your lot, its base zoning already sets the box you can build in. Scottsdale single-family districts run from R1-7 on small lots up to R1-190 on parcels of an acre and more, and each one carries its own yards. R1-7 wants a 20 foot front, a 5 foot side, and a 25 foot rear yard. R1-43, a common large-lot district, wants 40, 20, and 35 feet, all under a flat 30 foot height cap. That district is your starting point for how much house fits, and an overlay can only shrink it from there. For every district's exact setbacks, height, and lot coverage, see Scottsdale residential zoning and setbacks . For corner lots, also see corner lot and double frontage setbacks in Arizona .

The ESL ordinance and desert preservation

This is the rule that reshapes the acre you thought you bought. Scottsdale's Environmentally Sensitive Lands Ordinance (ESL) is a zoning overlay stretched across 134 square miles of north and east Scottsdale desert and mountains, and it requires every lot in it to set aside a share of land, forever, as Natural Area Open Space (NAOS). That set-aside runs from about 15% on flat lower desert up to 80% on steep hillside, and it carves out your buildable area before you draw a single wall. Riding along with it are a Native Plant Ordinance and plant-salvage rules that land right in your site plan.

ESL is the number-one reason a Scottsdale lot rarely buys you the house its raw acreage suggests. Picture a steep parcel where 80% has to stay open desert: only a fifth of the ground is buildable, and that fifth still has to hold the house, the drive, and the pool inside the setbacks. And this is not a one-time review you clear and forget. The NAOS is recorded against the lot, so it stays with the land permanently. Native plants standing in the build path have to be salvaged and either kept or moved, and any open space you disturb has to be replanted back to a city standard. Read the Scottsdale ESL ordinance for how the open-space percentage is calculated, and how ESL and community design review work together for the two-layer approval. For the on-the-ground steps, see NAOS revegetation rules and plant salvage and cactus boxing before grading .

Hillside and Foothills overlay lots

The view that sold you on a hillside lot is also what makes it the hardest and priciest ground to build on. Slope drives two things at once: the ESL open-space percentage and the city's Foothills overlay limits. The steeper the parcel, the more of it has to stay protected desert, and the tougher the work gets. Cut, fill, retaining walls, and engineered foundations all pile cost onto a hillside build. The hillside disturbance limits are laid out in the ESL ordinance page , and the dollar impact shows up in how much it costs to build a custom home in Scottsdale .

Inspections and certificate of occupancy

Scottsdale checks the work in stages, foundation to framing to final, and each stage has to pass before the next part gets closed up. Once the final inspection clears with no code violations, the city issues a Certificate of Occupancy, the paper that lets you legally move in. On many lots there is an earlier checkpoint too: a pre-site inspection before any grading begins. See the Scottsdale inspections process , residential building permit requirements , and what the Scottsdale pre-site inspection checks . The finish line is the certificate of occupancy .

Community design guidelines (Troon North)

In a lot of north Scottsdale luxury communities, the city is not the first door you have to get through. Places like Troon North run their own Architectural Review Committee that signs off on your design before the city ever sees it, and its rules often bite harder than the code. Troon North sets every home inside a recorded Building Envelope, keeps the desert around it untouched under a Natural Area Easement, and dictates your paint color (a 14 to 40 LRV earth-tone range), your fill, and your wall heights. Design to these rules from the first sketch, because reworking a set to satisfy them later is expensive. See Troon North design guidelines , and for the broader north-Scottsdale color rule, LRV paint color rules in north Scottsdale luxury communities . The building envelope and NAOS easement glossary entry explains the recorded-easement concept.

Buying the lot and starting the build

The smartest work on a Scottsdale home happens before you sign for the land. Check the lot's zoning, its overlay, and its slope first, because those three together decide your buildable area and your budget. A flat R1-43 lot and a steep ESL hillside parcel of the very same size can hand you two completely different houses. Get the land right and the rest runs through design, permitting, and construction in a sequence you can count on. See how to buy land to build a house in Arizona , how to know if your lot is in the city or unincorporated county , and the steps to building a custom home in Arizona . For the design stage, see what happens during the design phase of a custom home .

Site prep: grading, dust, and drainage

Before a footing pours on a Scottsdale lot, you usually need a grading or earthwork permit, a dust-control permit, and a drainage clearance, and an ESL lot piles plant salvage on top of all that. Treat the desert site work as its own phase with its own permits, because skipping any one step can bring the whole build to a halt. See grading or earthwork permit and SWPPP , dust-control permit , and drainage clearance and the Maricopa County grading matrix . Call 811 Blue Stake before digging , and budget for temporary construction power .

Choosing a builder and financing

Pick your licensed builder and set up a construction-to-permanent loan before the design locks, because the lender's appraisal and draw schedule end up steering the timeline. Check the contractor's license with the Arizona Registrar of Contractors (ROC) , and read every bid for what it actually includes, not just the number at the bottom. The cheapest bid is often the one leaving the most off the page. See how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC , what questions to ask a custom home builder before hiring , and how to compare custom home builder bids in Arizona . On financing, see construction-to-permanent loan in Arizona and construction loan requirements in Arizona .

Common Scottsdale questions

Three topics come up again and again in Scottsdale: the codes, casitas, and the ESL desert rules. Start with what building codes apply in Scottsdale for the plain-language answer on codes. Thinking about a guest space? See ADU and casita rules in Scottsdale and whether you can rent out a casita or ADU in Scottsdale . For the green-building track on city projects, see the Scottsdale Green Building Program . And for design that holds up to the heat, see how to design a custom home to stay cool in the Arizona heat .

Sources

The framing facts in this guide come from the City of Scottsdale: Building Code Information , the Environmentally Sensitive Lands Overlay (ESL) , and the city's Codes and Ordinances index . Arizona's broader code-adoption picture is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona .

This guide is general information, not a code or zoning interpretation. Fees, editions, and overlay rules change, so confirm the current numbers and requirements for your parcel with Scottsdale Planning and Development before you rely on them.

Sources

  • City of Scottsdale Building Code Information
  • City of Scottsdale Environmentally Sensitive Lands Overlay (ESL)
  • City of Scottsdale Codes and Ordinances
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona

title: Building a Custom Home on Rural Arizona Land: The Complete Guide
summary: Building on rural Arizona land means solving the things a city lot already has: water, a septic system, power and utility access, fire protection without a hydrant, and a foundation matched to the soil. On unincorporated parcels like Rio Verde Foothills you may rely on a private well, a hauled-water cistern, or a water district, each with its own permits, costs, and financing limits. Sorting water, septic, soils, and access before you design is what makes a rural build work.
bodyHtml:
Building a Custom Home on Rural Arizona Land: The Complete Guide

On a city lot, the water is already at the curb and the sewer is already in the street. Out on raw Arizona land, none of that is waiting for you. You bring it. That means finding water, putting in a septic system, running power to the site, protecting the house from fire with no hydrant anywhere near, and pouring a foundation the soil will actually hold. Take a parcel out in Rio Verde Foothills. Your water might come from a private well, from hauled water trucked in and stored in a cistern, or from a domestic water improvement district, and each of those roads has its own permits, its own price, and its own limits on financing. The dirt itself brings questions too: expansive soil and drainage decide the foundation, county rules govern the septic, and in brushy country you owe a cleared buffer of defensible space. This guide lays out the whole rural picture and links every piece to a page that goes deeper, so you can settle water, septic, soils, and access long before a design gets finalized.

What this guide covers

  • Rural land and what it changes
  • Where your water comes from
  • Drilling a well
  • Hauled water and cisterns
  • Water districts and shared wells
  • Testing and treating rural water
  • Septic systems
  • Power and utility access
  • Fire protection without a hydrant
  • Soils and foundation
  • Lot splits and the financing catch

Rural land and what it changes

First question, before anything else: is your land inside a city, or out in unincorporated county? The answer sets which rules you follow and which services simply are not there. Out in the county, the county is your building authority, city water and sewer usually do not reach you, and the water supply, the wastewater, and often the utility hookup all become your job. That quietly moves a big chunk of the budget into site work, spent before the house itself so much as starts.

Confirm your jurisdiction with How do I know if my lot is in the city or unincorporated county in Arizona? and learn the buying process in How do I buy land to build a house in Arizona? . For the rural-specific checklist, see What do I need to build on rural land in Rio Verde, Arizona? . For the rest of the build around this site work, see the master guide on how to build a custom home in Arizona .

Where your water comes from

Everything hinges on where the water comes from. That one choice drives your permits, your cost, your financing, and sometimes whether the county will approve the build at all. You have three common roads: a private well, hauled water in a cistern, or a domestic water improvement district or another piped provider. And in some areas the county wants proof of an adequate or assured water supply before it hands you a permit. So water is not just a utility you plumb in later. It can be the gate you have to clear to build.

Start with the overview in Building in Rio Verde Foothills water rules and How to get water to a home in Rio Verde Foothills . Whether you can build with no city water at all is answered in Can you build a custom home in Rio Verde Foothills without city water? . For the permit gate, see Do you need a well permit or adequate water supply determination to build in Maricopa County? and Can ADWR or Maricopa County deny a building permit over water in Arizona? .

Drilling a well

Drilling your own well sounds simple until you learn nobody can tell you how deep you will have to go. It is a common rural water source, but it takes a state filing, a licensed driller, and a certain tolerance for the unknown. In Arizona you file a notice of intent to drill with the Department of Water Resources, then you chase the water table, which sits at a different depth in every area and can run hundreds of feet down. An exempt domestic well comes with limits on how much it can pump. And a well that runs dry or drops over time may have to be deepened years later, on your dime.

See the filing in What is an ADWR notice of intent to drill a well? , depth in How deep do you have to drill a well in Arizona? , and the fix when it falls short in How much does it cost to deepen a well in Arizona? . The reference library carries the statutes: A.R.S. 45-454 (exempt domestic well) and A.R.S. 45-596 (well drilling notice) .

Hauled water and cisterns

When a well does not pencil out, plenty of rural homes just have the water trucked in. That is hauled water, delivered by tanker and stored in a cistern on your property. It works fine, but it changes what you plan for. You budget for delivery month after month, you size a tank big enough to carry you through a gap between trucks, and you make peace with the fact that some loan programs will not finance a hauled-water home. Tank size is a genuine design decision, a trade between how often the truck comes and how much you want to spend, and give up in yard space, on storage.

See the system in How big a water storage tank do I need for a hauled-water home in Arizona? and the ongoing cost in How much does it cost to haul water in Rio Verde Foothills? . Financing limits are covered in Can you finance a hauled-water or cistern home with a USDA or VA loan in Arizona? . The glossary defines hauled water and cistern .

Water districts and shared wells

There is a third road, and it often lends and resells the best of the three. You get organized water, either through a domestic water improvement district or a shared-well agreement with the neighbors, and either one can hand a rural lot a steadier, more financeable source. A water district is a public entity that can build the pipes and bill you for service. A shared well splits a single well among a few properties under a recorded agreement that lays out who pays what and who fixes what. Both tend to help a home's resale and its financing more than hauled water does.

Learn the district model in What is a domestic water improvement district, and how does it work in Arizona? and the neighbor arrangement in What is a shared well agreement in Arizona? . How the source affects value and lending is in Does a well or hauled water affect a Rio Verde Foothills property value and financing? .

Testing and treating rural water

Clear water is not the same as clean water. Well and hauled supplies can carry minerals, bacteria, or contaminants that a city system would have already stripped out, so you test before you trust it and you often treat it before it feels right at the tap. Have a new well checked for bacteria, nitrates, and the rest, and in some areas it is worth adding a PFAS test to the list. Hard water shows up all over Arizona, which is why so many rural homes run a whole-house softener or treatment system.

See the testing protocol in How and why do you test well water on a new rural Arizona home? and the emerging contaminant question in Do I need to test for PFAS in my Arizona well water? . On treatment, see Do I need a whole-house water softener or treatment system on a new Arizona home? .

Septic systems

No city sewer means the waste stays on your land, in an on-site septic system. The county will not permit one until it knows two things: how the soil drains and how big the house is. That first one comes from a perc test, which times how fast the ground soaks up water and sets the system type and the size of the drainfield. The system also has to keep its distance from any well or water source, by required setbacks. Get the septic designed and permitted early, because the results can move where the house and the well are even allowed to sit on the lot.

See the well-to-septic spacing in How close can a well be to a septic system in Arizona? . The county rules are in the reference library at Maricopa County septic and well requirements and Pinal County septic and well requirements . The glossary defines septic , the perc test , and graywater reuse.

Power and utility access

The power line you take for granted might be a quarter mile from where you want the house. On rural land, closing that gap can be one of the biggest line items in the whole project. Running electric service, and sometimes gas or communications, can cost anywhere from a modest sum to tens of thousands of dollars, all depending on distance and terrain, and it is enough of a number that some owners skip the pole entirely and go solar with battery storage. Either way, you will need a will-serve letter or service commitment, plus temporary construction power to run tools while the house goes up.

See the cost in How much does it cost to extend power or utilities to a rural Arizona lot? . The utility-commitment and temporary-power steps are detailed in the pre-construction permit layer guide , including What is a will-serve letter, and do I need one to build in Arizona? .

Fire protection without a hydrant

There is no hydrant on the corner out here, but the fire code still expects your home to be defended. It gets met a few ways: on-site water the fire crew can pull from, defensible space, and sometimes sprinklers. The code can call for a storage tank sized to deliver fire flow, roads a fire engine can actually drive and turn on, and a cleared buffer around the house where the brush is thick. All of that reaches back into the early decisions, shaping your site plan, how much water you store, and even how you landscape.

See how it is met in How does a rural Arizona home with no fire hydrant meet fire protection? and the clearance rule in What is defensible space, and how much do I need for an Arizona home? . The glossary defines defensible space .

Soils and foundation

The ground you build on has opinions, and in rural Arizona they are strong ones. Expansive clay swells when it gets wet and shrinks when it dries. Collapsible soils drop under load. Either one can dictate your entire foundation, which is why a soils report is one of the first things worth commissioning. A geotechnical report reads the ground for your engineer, so the foundation can be built to fight the heave and the settling, and on expansive soil that often means a post-tension slab or a deepened footing. Skip this step and you learn about the soil the hard way, through cracks. That is why most lenders and engineers want the report in hand before the design is locked.

Start with Do I need a soil test to build a house in Arizona? , then the deeper Do I need a soils or geotechnical report, and what triggers special foundation requirements in Arizona? . For the engineering response, see Foundation design for expansive soil in Arizona . The glossary defines expansive soil .

Lot splits and the financing catch

A cheap rural parcel can carry a hidden history, and that history can stop your build cold. Many of these lots exist because someone divided a bigger piece of land through a lot split, and when that split skipped the rules, a so-called "wildcat" split, the trouble surfaces years later. A wildcat split carves up land without going through the formal subdivision process, which can leave a parcel with no guaranteed water source and no proper legal access, and that snarls both permits and financing. So before you buy, dig into the parcel's split history and its water status. It can decide whether you are allowed to build, or able to finance, at all.

See What is a wildcat lot split in Arizona, and how does it affect water and permits? . For how the water source ties back to your loan, revisit Does a well or hauled water affect a Rio Verde Foothills property value and financing? and the full construction financing guide .

The takeaway

A rural build is won or lost on the ground work, not the granite. Water, septic, power, fire protection, soils, and a clean lot history all get settled before you ever design the house. Each section above links to a page with the live costs, permits, and county rules. Nail down your water path and the parcel's split history first, get the soils report done early, and put real money for the utility and fire-protection work in the budget from the start. For the rest of the journey, see the master guide on how to build a custom home in Arizona , and for the site permits that come with grading raw land, the pre-construction permit layer guide .

Sources

  • Arizona Department of Water Resources: Wells and Adequate Water Supply
  • Maricopa County Environmental Services: Onsite Wastewater (Septic) Forms and Applications
  • Arizona Department of Environmental Quality: Private well water testing

title: Choosing a Custom Home Builder in Arizona: The Complete Guide
summary: Choosing a custom home builder in Arizona starts with verifying an active ROC license and bond, then narrows through references, past homes, and apples-to-apples bid comparison before you sign a contract. The contract type (cost-plus, fixed-price, or guaranteed maximum price), the allowance structure, and the price-change and communication terms decide how the project actually runs. This guide maps the whole vetting and contracting journey with a deep page behind each step.
bodyHtml:
Choosing a Custom Home Builder in Arizona: The Complete Guide

You are about to hand one person a year of your life and a sum of money that could buy several nice cars. That is what picking a builder really is, so it pays to get it right. Everything in this guide flows from a single first move: make sure the builder holds an active Registrar of Contractors (ROC) license in the right class and carries a license bond. Skip that and you are exposed, because A.R.S. 32-1151 makes contracting without a license flat-out illegal, and an unlicensed builder cannot even take you to court to enforce a contract. Once the license checks out, you tighten the field: call references, walk past homes, and put every bid on the same scope so the numbers actually compare. Then you sign a written contract, which Arizona law (A.R.S. 32-1158) requires for residential work anyway. The kind of contract you sign, and its terms for allowances, price changes, and communication, is what decides how the whole build feels once it starts. This guide walks you through all of it and links the deep page behind every step.

What this guide covers

  • Verify the ROC license and bond first
  • Builder vs. general contractor vs. owner-builder
  • Questions to ask and the green and red flags
  • Checking references and touring past homes
  • Approved-builder lists in gated communities
  • Comparing bids apples to apples
  • The contract type: cost-plus, fixed, or GMP
  • Allowances, overages, and price changes
  • What the written contract must include
  • Communication, site visits, and the client portal

Verify the ROC license and bond first

This is the five-minute check that saves some people their entire project, so do it before anything else. An Arizona builder has to hold an active ROC license in the right class, the residential B class for a custom home, and post a license bond. You do not have to take anyone's word for it. Look the contractor up on the ROC website, and you can see whether the license is current, what class it covers, and whether there are complaints or discipline in the file. If there is no license, stop right there. A.R.S. 32-1151 bans unlicensed contracting, and an unlicensed contractor cannot even sue to collect payment, which tells you exactly how seriously the state treats it.

Start with how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC . The reference library carries A.R.S. 32-1151 (contracting without a license) , A.R.S. 32-1102 (license classifications) , and A.R.S. 32-1152 (the license bond) . The glossary defines the ROC , the contractor's license , and the contractor bond .

Builder vs. general contractor vs. owner-builder

The word "builder" gets thrown around loosely, and the differences matter because each role carries a different level of risk for you. It can mean a custom home builder, a general contractor, or you, wearing the hard hat as your own owner-builder. A custom home builder designs and builds and puts their name behind the finished home. A general contractor runs the trades. An owner-builder is you, the homeowner, stepping into the contractor's role and shouldering the contractor's liability. Going the owner-builder route can save you the builder's margin, sure. It also drops permits, scheduling, lien risk, and warranty squarely on your shoulders, which is a lot to carry on a first build.

Compare the roles in custom home builder vs. general contractor . If you are tempted to self-manage, read can I be my own general contractor in Arizona? . The glossary defines the owner-builder and the build-on-your-lot model many custom builders use.

Questions to ask and the green and red flags

Ask every builder the same set of questions, and keep one eye open for the signals that mark a shaky or overbooked one. The good signs are easy to spot: an active license, references who actually pick up the phone, a clear contract, a portfolio full of homes like the one you want, and a payment schedule laid out in plain terms. The warning signs are the mirror image. No license. A demand for a big cash deposit up front. No written contract. A push to break ground before the paperwork is done. Any one of those should slow you down.

Build your interview with what questions should I ask a custom home builder before hiring? . Learn the positive signs in what are the green flags of a good custom home builder? and the warning signs in red flags a custom home builder is not legitimate in Arizona .

Checking references and touring past homes

A glossy photo hides everything that actually matters, so get in the car and go see the work. Photos cannot show you build quality, follow-through, or how a builder acts when something goes sideways. Call past clients and ask the pointed question, not the polite one: what went wrong, and how did the builder handle it? Then walk two things in person. A finished home tells you the level of finish. A job in progress tells you how the crew works day to day. And find a home that is a few years old, because that is where you see whether the work held up after a few Arizona summers.

Vet the references with how do I check a custom home builder's references and past work? . Know what to look at on a tour with what should I look for when touring a custom builder's past homes? .

Approved-builder lists in gated communities

Imagine falling for a builder, signing the contract, and then learning the community will not let them through the gate. It happens. If your lot sits in a gated or guard-gated Arizona community, check for an approved-builder list before you get attached to anyone. Some luxury communities in North Scottsdale, Cave Creek, and Fountain Hills only allow builders who are pre-qualified, bonded to the HOA, or already fluent in the design guidelines. That can shrink your list of options, so find out early in the search, not the day after you sign.

See how these lists work in do I have to use an approved-builder list in a gated Arizona community? .

Comparing bids apples to apples

The lowest bid is often the most expensive one, and here is why. That tempting bottom-line number frequently leans on thin allowances and quietly leaves out line items, which come roaring back as change orders once the work is underway. So do not compare headline prices. Compare identical scope. Hand every builder the same plans, the same spec level, and the same allowance assumptions, then look at what each one actually includes. Nine times out of ten, a higher bid with honest allowances finishes cheaper than a lowball bid that was built to be beaten by overages.

Compare correctly with how do I compare custom home builder bids and quotes in Arizona? . The allowance trap is explained in what is a builder allowance, and what happens if you go over? . The glossary defines the allowance and the change order .

The contract type: cost-plus, fixed, or GMP

Your contract type is really a decision about who eats the risk if costs run over, so choose it on purpose. The three main kinds split that risk in very different ways. Cost-plus pays the builder's actual costs plus a fee, which puts the overrun risk on you. Fixed-price shifts that risk to the builder, though they cushion themselves with a contingency baked into the number. A guaranteed maximum price (GMP) splits the difference: it caps what you can pay while sharing any savings if the job comes in under. Which one fits comes down to two things. How finished are your plans, and how much price certainty do you need to sleep at night?

Weigh the structures with cost-plus vs. fixed-price home contract , the detail of cost-plus custom home contract , and what is a guaranteed maximum price (GMP) contract? . The glossary defines cost-plus and guaranteed maximum price .

Allowances, overages, and price changes

This is where the final number quietly drifts away from the one you signed, so nail down how allowances, overages, and price increases work before you agree to anything. An allowance is a placeholder dollar amount for a choice you have not made yet, say the tile or the light fixtures. Pick something pricier than the allowance and the difference lands on your bill, and how that difference gets charged is not the same on a cost-plus contract as on a fixed-price one. The good news: Arizona limits how freely a builder can bump a fixed price after you sign. The catch: allowance overages and change orders are still the fair, legal ways a price climbs, and they climb quietly if you are not watching.

Understand overage billing with how are builder allowance overages billed on a cost-plus vs. fixed-price contract? . Know your protection against arbitrary increases in can a home builder raise the price after you sign in Arizona? . If you want to provide your own fixtures, see can I supply my own materials or fixtures for my custom home? .

What the written contract must include

If it is not in writing, it does not exist. Put everything in the contract, both because Arizona law (A.R.S. 32-1158) requires a written agreement for residential construction and because that document is what governs scope, price, schedule, and what you can do if things go wrong. A complete custom home contract spells it all out: the plans and specs, the price and the payment or draw schedule, the allowances, how change orders get handled, the warranty terms, and the date it is all supposed to be done. A vague contract, or no contract at all, is a red flag on its own, and it leaves you standing alone the moment a dispute shows up.

Build a complete agreement with what to include in a custom home contract . The reference library carries A.R.S. 32-1158 (home construction contract requirements) and A.R.S. 32-1158.02 (residential construction contract) .

Communication, site visits, and the client portal

Pay close attention to how a builder talks to you, because a year-long project rides on steady, honest updates far more than on any single clause in the contract. The best builders keep a rhythm you can count on: one clear point of contact, regular progress updates, walkthroughs at the big milestones, and often a client portal that gathers the schedule, your selections, the draws, and jobsite photos in one place. Settle the communication rhythm before you sign, and get clear on how often you are welcome on site.

Set expectations with how important is communication with your builder during a custom build? and how often should I visit my custom home during construction? . Evaluate the software with what should a custom builder's client portal or project management software show you? .

The takeaway

Boil it all down and choosing a builder is a funnel. Check the ROC license and bond. Watch for the good signs and the bad ones. Call references and walk real homes. Put every bid on equal scope. Then sign a written contract whose type and allowance terms fit how much price certainty you need. Every section above links to the deep page with the specific Arizona rules and steps. Start with the license, never skip the written contract, and pick someone who keeps you in the loop, and you will have done the hardest and most important work of the whole project before a single footing is poured. For your protection after move-in, see our warranty, defects, and disputes guide .

Sources

  • Arizona Registrar of Contractors (ROC): verify a license and check before you hire
  • A.R.S. 32-1151 (contracting without a license is prohibited)
  • A.R.S. 32-1158 (written contract requirements for residential construction)
